# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. What conclusion do you draw if all elements of the first column of the Routh array have the same sign?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Routh-Hurwitz criterion states that the **number of roots** of the characteristic equation lying in the **right-half of the s-plane** is equal to the **number of sign changes** in the **first column** of the Routh array.

If all elements of the first column have the **same sign** (all positive or all negative), there are **zero sign changes**, which means the characteristic equation has **no roots in the RHP**. The system is therefore **stable or marginally stable**. To distinguish between these two cases, one must check for any **purely imaginary roots** (typically via an auxiliary equation). If the characteristic equation has **no roots on the $j\omega$-axis**, the system is **asymptotically stable**; if it has **simple (non-repeated) roots on the $j\omega$-axis**, it is **marginally stable**.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: What is an auxiliary equation in the Routh array? When does it arise and how is it used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **auxiliary equation** is a polynomial $A(s)$ constructed from the row **immediately above** a **row of zeros** in the Routh array, using only the **even powers of $s$**.

It arises during Routh array construction when an **entire row becomes zero**, which is a **Special Case II** situation indicating the presence of:
- Symmetric roots about the origin, or
- Pairs of purely imaginary roots, or
- Complex-conjugate quadruples symmetric about the origin.

**How it is used:**
1. Form $A(s)$ from the coefficients of the row above the zero row (even powers of $s$ only).
2. **Differentiate** $A(s)$ with respect to $s$ to obtain $dA(s)/ds$.
3. **Replace** the row of zeros with the coefficients of $dA(s)/ds$.
4. Continue the array construction.

The **roots of $A(s) = 0$** are themselves **roots of the characteristic equation** and lie **symmetrically about the origin**. This procedure is essential for determining whether the system is marginally stable or unstable in the presence of symmetric roots.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts, typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice is provided as two completely independent alternatives (Q-A or Q-B).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ CHOICE 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q-A:** **(a)** For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$, determine the **range of $K$** for which the closed-loop system is stable. Also find the **frequency of oscillation** at the critical value of $K$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is $s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$. Comment on the number of roots in the right-half s-plane. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Form the characteristic equation.

The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + G(s) = 0$:

$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

Expanding: $s(s^2 + 6s + 8) + K = 0$

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Build the Routh array.

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 8 \\
s^2 & 6 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{48 - K}{6} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

Element computation: $b_1 = \dfrac{6 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot K}{6} = \dfrac{48 - K}{6}$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the stability conditions.

For stability, all first-column elements must be positive:
- $1 > 0$ ‚úď
- $6 > 0$ ‚úď
- $\dfrac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \ \Rightarrow\ K < 48$
- $K > 0$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion.

$$\boxed{0 < K < 48}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Frequency of oscillation at the critical value $K = 48$.

At $K = 48$, the $s^1$ row becomes zero. The auxiliary equation is the $s^2$ row:

$$6s^2 + 48 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ s^2 = -8 \ \Rightarrow\ s = \pm j 2\sqrt{2}$$

Frequency of oscillation: $\omega = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$ rad/s.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the characteristic equation.

$$D(s) = s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Check the necessary condition.

All coefficients $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ are present, real, and positive ‚úď.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Build the Routh array.

- $s^4$: $1, \ 3, \ 5$
- $s^3$: $2, \ 4, \ 0$

$$s^2: \ b_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 4}{2} = 1, \quad b_2 = \frac{2 \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 0}{2} = 5$$

$s^2$ row: $1, \ 5$

$$s^1: \ c_1 = \frac{1 \cdot 4 - 2 \cdot 5}{1} = -6, \quad c_2 = \frac{1 \cdot 0 - 2 \cdot 0}{1} = 0$$

$s^1$ row: $-6, \ 0$

$$s^0: \ d_1 = \frac{(-6) \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 0}{-6} = 5$$

$s^0$ row: $5$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Assemble the array.

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 3 & 5 \\
s^3 & 2 & 4 & 0 \\
s^2 & 1 & 5 & 0 \\
s^1 & -6 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & 5 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Count sign changes in the first column.

First column: $1, \ 2, \ 1, \ -6, \ 5$

Signs: $+, +, +, -, +$ $\Rightarrow$ **2 sign changes**.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion.

The characteristic equation has **2 roots in the right-half s-plane**. The system is **UNSTABLE**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ CHOICE 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q-B:** **(a)** Construct the Routh array and determine the stability of the system with characteristic equation $s^5 + s^4 + 2s^3 + 2s^2 + 3s + 5 = 0$. Identify and handle any special cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has the characteristic equation $s^4 + s^3 + 5s^2 + 3s + 6 = 0$. Determine whether the system is **stable, marginally stable, or unstable**. If marginally stable, find the frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the characteristic equation.

$$D(s) = s^5 + s^4 + 2s^3 + 2s^2 + 3s + 5 = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Build the first two rows.

- $s^5$: $1, \ 2, \ 3$
- $s^4$: $1, \ 2, \ 5$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the $s^3$ row.

$$b_1 = \frac{1 \cdot 2 - 1 \cdot 2}{1} = 0, \quad b_2 = \frac{1 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 5}{1} = -2$$

$s^3$ row: $0, \ -2, \ 0$ ‚ÄĒ **first element is zero**.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the **$\varepsilon$-substitution (Special Case I)**.

Replace the zero with $\varepsilon > 0$:

$s^3$ row: $\varepsilon, \ -2, \ 0$

Compute the $s^2$ row:

$$c_1 = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot 2 - 1 \cdot (-2)}{\varepsilon} = \frac{2\varepsilon + 2}{\varepsilon} = 2 + \frac{2}{\varepsilon}$$

$$c_2 = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 0}{\varepsilon} = 5$$

As $\varepsilon \to 0^+$: $c_1 = 2 + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} \to +\infty$.

$s^2$ row: $+\infty, \ 5, \ 0$ (treating $+\infty$ as a large positive).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the remaining rows and the first column.

First column: $1, \ 1, \ 0^+, \ +\infty, \ -2, \ \ldots$

Signs: $+, +, +, +, -, \ldots$ $\Rightarrow$ **1 sign change**.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion.

The system has **1 root in the right-half s-plane** $\Rightarrow$ **UNSTABLE**.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the characteristic equation.

$$s^4 + s^3 + 5s^2 + 3s + 6 = 0$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Build the Routh array up to the special case.

- $s^4$: $1, \ 5, \ 6$
- $s^3$: $1, \ 3, \ 0$

$$s^2: \ b_1 = \frac{1 \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 3}{1} = 2, \quad b_2 = \frac{1 \cdot 6 - 1 \cdot 0}{1} = 6$$

$s^2$ row: $2, \ 6, \ 0$

$$s^1: \ c_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 6}{2} = \frac{6 - 6}{2} = 0$$

$s^1$ row: $0, \ 0$ ‚ÄĒ **entire row of zeros (Special Case II)**.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Form the auxiliary equation from the row above (the $s^2$ row).

$$A(s) = 2s^2 + 6 = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Differentiate $A(s)$.

$$\frac{dA(s)}{ds} = 4s + 0 = 4s$$

Coefficients: $4, \ 0$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Replace the row of zeros with $dA/ds$ coefficients and continue.

$s^1$ row: $4, \ 0$

$$s^0: \ d_1 = \frac{4 \cdot 6 - 2 \cdot 0}{4} = 6$$

$s^0$ row: $6$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final array and sign-change count.

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 5 & 6 \\
s^3 & 1 & 3 & 0 \\
s^2 & 2 & 6 & 0 \\
s^1 & 4 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & 6 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

First column: $1, \ 1, \ 2, \ 4, \ 6$ ‚ÄĒ all positive $\Rightarrow$ **0 sign changes**.

**No RHP roots.**

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Solve the auxiliary equation and conclude.

$$2s^2 + 6 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ s^2 = -3 \ \Rightarrow\ s = \pm j\sqrt{3}$$

These are **simple (non-repeated) imaginary roots** $\Rightarrow$ **MARGINALLY STABLE**.

**Frequency of oscillation:** $\omega = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ rad/s.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚ÄĒ Common Mark-Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to append a zero** when the second row has fewer elements than the first. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 2. **Confusing the two special cases.** A zero in the *first column* alone $\Rightarrow$ use $\varepsilon$. An *entire row* of zeros $\Rightarrow$ use the auxiliary equation. Swapping these is a **2-3 mark deduction**.
> 3. **Concluding "stable" without checking the auxiliary equation.** Zero sign changes only rules out RHP roots. You **must** check for $j\omega$-axis roots to distinguish stable from marginally stable. *(Lose 1-2 marks)*
> 4. **Sign errors in the determinant formula.** Always verify: $\text{new} = \dfrac{(\text{upper} \times \text{upper-upper}) - (\text{side} \times \text{upper-side})}{\text{upper}}$. A single arithmetic slip cascades through the entire array.
> 5. **Not stating the frequency of oscillation** at the marginal stability point. Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for this in 14-mark problems.
> 6. **For unity-feedback problems, forgetting to expand** $1 + G(s) = 0$ into the characteristic equation. Always show this first step.
> 7. **Concluding the system is marginally stable when a row-of-zeros yields repeated imaginary roots.** Repeated $j\omega$-axis roots $\Rightarrow$ **UNSTABLE**, not marginally stable. This is the trickiest KTU trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Routh-Hurwitz** is an **algebraic** stability test ‚ÄĒ no root-finding required.
- The **characteristic equation** is the **starting point** for every Routh analysis.
- The **first column** of the array is the only column that matters for the **sign-change count**.
- **Necessary condition** (not sufficient): all coefficients real, present, same sign.
- **First row** ($s^n$): odd-indexed coefficients; **second row** ($s^{n-1}$): even-indexed coefficients.
- **New element formula** (determinant form):

$$b = \frac{c_1 d_2 - c_2 d_1}{c_1}$$

- **Special Case I** (zero in first column): substitute $\varepsilon \to 0^+$.
- **Special Case II** (entire row of zeros): form auxiliary equation from row above (even powers of $s$), differentiate, replace the zero row.
- **Number of RHP roots = number of sign changes** in the first column.
- **Stable:** no RHP roots AND no $j\omega$-axis roots.
- **Marginally stable:** no RHP roots, but simple roots on $j\omega$-axis (sustained oscillation).
- **Unstable:** at least one RHP root OR repeated $j\omega$-axis roots.
- For **unity-feedback** problems, the characteristic equation is $1 + G(s) = 0$, **expanded fully** before applying Routh.
- The **range of $K$ for stability** is the most common 14-mark KTU problem pattern.
- The **auxiliary equation's roots** give the **oscillation frequency** at the **critical (marginal) value of $K$**.
- Always **end the solution** with a clear **conclusion statement**: stable / marginally stable / unstable, with the value of $K$ range or oscillation frequency as required.
- **Memorize the layout** of the array ‚ÄĒ most KTU valuation errors are layout errors, not concept errors.
- The criterion is named after **Edward John Routh** (English, 1877) and **Adolf Hurwitz** (German-Swiss, 1895), who arrived at the same conclusion **independently** ‚ÄĒ a beautiful example of simultaneous discovery in engineering mathematics.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behaviour of Energy Storage Elements in the Presence of Sources - Analysis of Source Free Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT412 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Behaviour of Energy Storage Elements in the Presence of Sources - Analysis of Source Free Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 ‚Äď Source-Free Networks & Energy Storage Element Behaviour

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Source-Free Network:** A network in which all *independent sources have been deactivated* (voltage sources ‚Üí short circuit; current sources ‚Üí open circuit), and the entire circuit response is sustained **solely by the initial energy stored** in inductors and capacitors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Natural Response (Transient Response):** The circuit's behaviour when energy initially stored in the reactive elements is allowed to dissipate through the resistive part of the network, without any external excitation.

> [!NOTE]
> **First-Order Circuit:** A circuit containing exactly **one** independent energy-storage element (L or C). It is governed by a **first-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)** of the form:
>
> $$\frac{dx(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} x(t) = 0$$

Where:
- $x(t)$ is the chosen state variable (current through L or voltage across C)
- $\tau$ is the **time constant** of the network (in seconds)

### 1.2 The Two Reactive Elements ‚Äď Fundamental Laws

| Element | Stores Energy As | Defining Equation | Continuity Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductor (L)** | Magnetic field: $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$ | $v_L = L\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Current **cannot** change instantaneously |
| **Capacitor (C)** | Electric field: $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2}Cv^2$ | $i_C = C\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | Voltage **cannot** change instantaneously |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuity Principles (State-Continuity):**
> - $i_L(0^-) = i_L(0^+)$
> - $v_C(0^-) = v_C(0^+)$
>
> These are the boundary conditions needed to solve any first-order ODE.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äď The Water Tank Intuition

Imagine a **water tank** with a small drain at the bottom:

- The **water level** = the stored energy in the reactive element.
- The **drain opening size** = the equivalent resistance in the network.
- The **rate at which water falls** = the rate of discharge (governed by a time constant $\tau$).

> A **wide drain + small tank** ‚Üí water rushes out **fast** ‚Üí **small $\tau$**.
> A **narrow drain + large tank** ‚Üí water leaks out **slowly** ‚Üí **large $\tau$**.

This is **exactly** how an inductor or capacitor behaves in a source-free network: energy bleeds away **exponentially** through the resistive path. The element with the largest "stored energy" relative to the available "drainage" path dictates how long the circuit "remembers" its initial state.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalised First-Order Source-Free Equation

Once a single source-free energy-storage element is reduced to its **Th√©venin equivalent** resistance $R_{Th}$ as seen from its terminals, the governing ODE is identical in form for both RL and RC cases.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The reactive element is the only source of energy.
2. The resistive network simply dissipates this energy.
3. The dissipation is governed by a first-order linear ODE.
4. The mathematical solution must be a function that equals its own derivative (up to a constant scaling) ‚ÄĒ this uniquely points to the **exponential function** $e^{-t/\tau}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | RL Source-Free Circuit | RC Source-Free Circuit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy element | Inductor $L$ | Capacitor $C$ |
| 2 | Equivalent resistance | $R_{Th}$ (Th√©venin seen by L) | $R_{Th}$ (Th√©venin seen by C) |
| 3 | **Time Constant** | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R_{Th}}$ **(seconds)** | $\tau = R_{Th} \cdot C$ **(seconds)** |
| 4 | Initial condition | $i_L(0^+) = I_0$ | $v_C(0^+) = V_0$ |
| 5 | Governing ODE | $L\dfrac{di}{dt} + Ri = 0$ | $RC\dfrac{dv}{dt} + v = 0$ |
| 6 | Natural current $i(t)$ | $I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\dfrac{V_0}{R}\, e^{-t/\tau}$ |
| 7 | Natural voltage $v(t)$ | $R I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | $V_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ |
| 8 | Stored energy | $W_L(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}L I_0^2 e^{-2t/\tau}$ | $W_C(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}C V_0^2 e^{-2t/\tau}$ |
| 9 | Time to decay to 1% of $I_0$ | $t \approx 4.6 \tau$ | $t \approx 4.6 \tau$ |
| 10 | Time to decay to 5% of $I_0$ | $t \approx 3 \tau$ | $t \approx 3 \tau$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (5ŌĄ rule):** After approximately **5 time constants**, the natural response is considered to have decayed to **less than 1%** of its initial value and is practically zero.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Systems:** Analysing de-energisation transients in transmission lines.
- **Electronics:** Understanding how RC filters shape pulses in digital circuits (snubber circuits, de-bouncing).
- **Control Systems:** Establishing the dominant time constant for stability analysis.
- **Automotive:** Modelling the discharge curve of a high-voltage capacitor in EV braking systems.
- **Aerospace:** Predicting how long a magnetic coil retains current after a relay is opened.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Source-Free RL Circuit

**Circuit Setup:** A resistor $R$ is connected in series with an inductor $L$ that carries an initial current $I_0 = i_L(0^-)$ at $t = 0$. The source is removed (or a switch is opened from the source side).

![Source-Free RL Circuit Concept: L in parallel with R (after source removal) or L in series with R after switch opens ‚ÄĒ both yield the same ODE via KVL/KCL]

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**

$$v_L(t) + v_R(t) = 0$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute element equations:**

$$L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + R \, i(t) = 0$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Rearrange into the standard form:**

$$\frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{R}{L} i(t) = 0$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Identify the time constant:**

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R}$$

So the ODE becomes:

$$\frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} i(t) = 0$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Separate variables and integrate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{di(t)}{i(t)} &= -\frac{1}{\tau}\, dt \\
\int_{I_0}^{i(t)} \frac{1}{i} \, di &= -\frac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{t} dt \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{i(t)}{I_0}\right) &= -\frac{t}{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Exponentiate both sides:**

$$
i(t) = I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}
$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Voltage across the resistor:**

$$v_R(t) = R \, i(t) = R I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Voltage across the inductor (using $v_L = L \frac{di}{dt}$):**

$$
v_L(t) = L \left(-\frac{I_0}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}\right) = -R I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign in $v_L$ confirms Lenz's Law ‚ÄĒ the inductor **opposes** the change in current and momentarily acts as a *source* to sustain current flow.

**Step 9 ‚ÄĒ Energy dissipated (sanity check):**

$$
W_R = \int_0^{\infty} i^2(t) R \, dt = I_0^2 R \int_0^{\infty} e^{-2t/\tau} dt = \frac{1}{2} L I_0^2
$$

This matches the initial stored energy ‚ÄĒ confirming **conservation of energy**. ‚úď

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Source-Free RC Circuit

**Circuit Setup:** A resistor $R$ is connected across a charged capacitor $C$ with initial voltage $V_0 = v_C(0^-)$ at $t = 0$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top node:**

$$i_C(t) + i_R(t) = 0$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute element equations:**

$$C \frac{dv(t)}{dt} + \frac{v(t)}{R} = 0$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Rearrange into standard form:**

$$\frac{dv(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{RC} v(t) = 0$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Identify the time constant:**

$$\tau = R C$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Solve the separable ODE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dv}{v} &= -\frac{1}{\tau}\, dt \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{v(t)}{V_0}\right) &= -\frac{t}{\tau} \\
v(t) &= V_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Current through the resistor:**

$$i(t) = \frac{v(t)}{R} = \frac{V_0}{R} \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Current through the capacitor (sign verification):**

$$i_C(t) = C \frac{dv}{dt} = -\frac{V_0}{R} \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Energy stored initially vs. dissipated:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_C(0) &= \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 \\
W_R &= \int_0^{\infty} \frac{v^2(t)}{R} \, dt = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \int_0^{\infty} e^{-2t/\tau} dt = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2
\end{aligned}
$$

All initial energy is dissipated ‚ÄĒ **energy conservation** verified. ‚úď

---

## 3.3 Generalised Solution Form (For Exam Quick-Write)

For **any first-order source-free network**:

$$
\boxed{\;x(t) = x(0^+)\, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad t \geq 0\;}
$$

where $x(0^+)$ is the **initial value of the state variable** (read from the circuit at $t = 0^-$ using continuity rules), and $\tau$ is the **Th√©venin-equivalent time constant** seen from the terminals of the single storage element.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm for Solving Source-Free First-Order Circuits

The following Mermaid block provides a **block-level functional architecture flow** describing the systematic procedure every KTU student must follow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Source-Free Circuit] --> B[Identify the Single Energy Storage Element L or C]
    B --> C{Is it an Inductor or Capacitor?}
    C -->|Inductor L| D[Step 1: Find Initial Current I0 = iL of 0 minus]
    C -->|Capacitor C| E[Step 1: Find Initial Voltage V0 = vC of 0 minus]
    D --> F[Step 2: Deactivate all Independent Sources V to short I to open]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step 3: Find RTh Seen from Terminals of L or C]
    G --> H{Compute Time Constant tau}
    H -->|Inductor| I[tau = L divided by RTh]
    H -->|Capacitor| J[tau = RTh times C]
    I --> K[Step 4: Write General Solution x of t = x0 times e raised to minus t by tau]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Step 5: Substitute Initial Condition x of 0 plus]
    L --> M[Step 6: Write Final Closed-Form Response i of t or v of t]
    M --> N[Step 7: Optional: Compute Stored or Dissipated Energy for Verification]
    N --> O[END: Natural Response Fully Characterised]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Block: RL vs RC Source-Free Decay

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RL [SOURCE-FREE RL]
        RL1[L with initial current I0] --> RL2[R dissipates energy]
        RL2 --> RL3[tau = L over R]
        RL3 --> RL4[Current: I0 e raised to minus t by tau]
    end
    subgraph RC [SOURCE-FREE RC]
        RC1[C with initial voltage V0] --> RC2[R dissipates energy]
        RC2 --> RC3[tau = R times C]
        RC3 --> RC4[Voltage: V0 e raised to minus t by tau]
    end
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic of Source-Free Discharge

```mermaid
graph TD
    SW[Switch opens at t=0 removing source] --> NODE{Top Node}
    NODE --> L[Inductor L with stored current I0]
    NODE --> R[Resistor R providing discharge path]
    L -.->|Energy bleeds through| R
    R --> GND[Reference Node / Ground]
    style L fill:#fff2cc
    style R fill:#dae8fc
    style SW fill:#f8cecc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ūüďė PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define a source-free network. Why does the natural response in such a network decay exponentially?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A source-free network is one in which all independent sources have been deactivated (voltage sources replaced by short circuits and current sources by open circuits), and the only energy driving the response is the **energy initially stored** in inductors and/or capacitors. The energy dissipates through the resistive part of the network, and the governing first-order ODE has the form $\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{x}{\tau} = 0$, whose unique solution is an **exponential decay** $x(t) = x(0^+)e^{-t/\tau}$. The decay is exponential because the instantaneous rate of energy loss is **proportional to the energy remaining** at that instant ‚ÄĒ a defining property of exponential processes. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**State the continuity conditions for current through an inductor and voltage across a capacitor. Why are they necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The continuity conditions are:

$$i_L(0^-) = i_L(0^+) \quad \text{and} \quad v_C(0^-) = v_C(0^+)$$

They arise because **infinite power** would be required to instantaneously change the energy stored in these elements. For an inductor, $p = v_L i_L$, and since $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$, an instantaneous current change would require infinite voltage, which is physically impossible. Similarly, a capacitor's voltage cannot jump because that would demand infinite current. These conditions provide the **initial conditions** required to uniquely solve the first-order ODEs governing the circuit. **(3 marks)**

---

## ūüďė PART B ‚ÄĒ Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ‚≠ź QUESTION A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Source-Free RL

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, Q from Module 3]**

In the circuit shown, the switch has been closed for a long time and is opened at $t = 0$. The circuit parameters are: $V_s = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 4\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 8\,\Omega$, and $L = 2\,\text{H}$.

**(a)** Find $i_L(0^+)$ and $v_L(0^+)$. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine $i_L(t)$ and $v_L(t)$ for $t \geq 0$. Also calculate the time constant $\tau$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution

**Part (a): Finding initial conditions [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** With the switch closed for a long time ($t < 0$), the inductor behaves as a **short circuit** (steady-state DC). Current $i_L(0^-)$ is therefore found using the simple resistive divider:

$$
i_L(0^-) = \frac{V_s}{R_1} = \frac{12}{4} = 3\,\text{A}
$$

*(Note: With the switch closed, $R_2$ is in parallel with the shorted inductor, so it carries no current ‚ÄĒ only $R_1$ limits the current.)*

**Step 2:** Apply the **continuity condition** for the inductor:

$$
i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-) = 3\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 3:** When the switch opens at $t = 0$, the source is disconnected. The inductor discharges through $R_1$ and $R_2$ in **series** (forming a loop with L). The Th√©venin resistance seen by the inductor is:

$$
R_{Th} = R_1 + R_2 = 4 + 8 = 12\,\Omega
$$

**Step 4:** At $t = 0^+$, by KVL around the source-free loop, the inductor's voltage must balance the resistor drop:

$$
v_L(0^+) = -i_L(0^+) \cdot R_{Th} = -3 \times 12 = -36\,\text{V}
$$

(The negative sign indicates the inductor acts as a source, opposing the change.)

**Part (a) final answers:** $i_L(0^+) = 3\,\text{A}$, $v_L(0^+) = -36\,\text{V}$ ‚ÄĒ **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b): Time constant and response [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Time constant:

$$
\tau = \frac{L}{R_{Th}} = \frac{2}{12} = \frac{1}{6}\,\text{s} \approx 0.1667\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** General form of the natural response:

$$
i_L(t) = i_L(0^+)\, e^{-t/\tau} = 3\, e^{-6t}\,\text{A}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Step 3:** Voltage across the inductor:

$$
v_L(t) = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = 2 \cdot \left[3 \cdot (-6)\, e^{-6t}\right] = -36\, e^{-6t}\,\text{V}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Part (b) final answers:**
- $\tau = 1/6$ s
- $i_L(t) = 3 e^{-6t}$ A
- $v_L(t) = -36 e^{-6t}$ V ‚ÄĒ **(7 marks)**

---

### ‚≠ź QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Source-Free RC (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, Q from Module 3]**

A capacitor $C = 100\,\mu\text{F}$ is initially charged to $V_0 = 50\,\text{V}$. At $t = 0$, it is connected across a parallel combination of $R_1 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**(a)** Find the equivalent resistance and the time constant of the circuit. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the voltage $v_C(t)$ across the capacitor for $t \geq 0$ and the time required for the capacitor to discharge to 5% of its initial voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution

**Part (a): Equivalent resistance and time constant [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** The two resistors are in **parallel**:

$$
R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{5 \times 20}{5 + 20} = \frac{100}{25} = 4\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2:** Time constant:

$$
\tau = R_{eq} \cdot C = 4 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.4\,\text{s}
$$

**Part (a) final answers:** $R_{eq} = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\tau = 0.4$ s ‚ÄĒ **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b): Voltage response and 5% time [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** The capacitor voltage in a source-free RC network follows:

$$
v_C(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t/\tau} = 50\, e^{-t/0.4} = 50\, e^{-2.5 t}\,\text{V}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Step 2:** Set $v_C(t) = 0.05 \cdot V_0 = 0.05 \times 50 = 2.5$ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.5 &= 50 \, e^{-2.5 t} \\
\frac{2.5}{50} &= e^{-2.5 t} \\
0.05 &= e^{-2.5 t} \\
\ln(0.05) &= -2.5 t \\
t &= \frac{-\ln(0.05)}{2.5} = \frac{2.9957}{2.5} \approx 1.198\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via 5ŌĄ rule:** $5\tau = 5 \times 0.4 = 2.0$ s ‚ÄĒ corresponds to $e^{-5} \approx 0.0067 \approx 0.67\%$ decay, which is less than 1% of $V_0$ (our exact 1.198 s is for 5%).

**Part (b) final answers:** $v_C(t) = 50 e^{-2.5t}$ V; $t \approx 1.198$ s ‚ÄĒ **(7 marks)**

---

## ‚ö†ÔłŹ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the continuity condition** (3‚Äď4 marks lost): Many students assume $i_L(0^+) = 0$ or $v_C(0^+) = 0$ without justification. **Always** state $i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-)$ and $v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-)$ explicitly.
>
> 2. **Wrong time constant formula** (2‚Äď3 marks lost): Confusing $\tau = L/R$ with $\tau = RC$. Remember: **$\tau = L/R$ for RL**, **$\tau = RC$ for RC**.
>
> 3. **Incorrect $R_{Th}$ identification** (4‚Äď5 marks lost): When resistors are in complex networks, students often fail to deactivate sources correctly. **Voltage sources ‚Üí short; Current sources ‚Üí open** *before* finding $R_{Th}$.
>
> 4. **Missing the negative sign in $v_L$** (1 mark lost): The voltage across an inductor during natural response is opposite in polarity to the conventional assumed direction ‚ÄĒ explicit sign matters in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Skipping units** (1 mark lost): Always carry units (H, F, ő©, s) in the final answer table.

---

## ūüéĮ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ‚úÖ A **source-free network** has no independent sources; energy comes only from initial conditions in L and C.
- ‚úÖ **Continuity rules:** $i_L(0^-) = i_L(0^+)$ and $v_C(0^-) = v_C(0^+)$.
- ‚úÖ **Time constant for RL:** $\tau = L / R_{Th}$ (units: seconds).
- ‚úÖ **Time constant for RC:** $\tau = R_{Th} \cdot C$ (units: seconds).
- ‚úÖ **Universal first-order natural response:** $x(t) = x(0^+) e^{-t/\tau}$.
- ‚úÖ **5ŌĄ rule:** Response is considered fully decayed after approximately **5 time constants** (<1% remains).
- ‚úÖ **Energy conservation:** Total initial stored energy = total energy dissipated in resistors.
- ‚úÖ **Deactivation protocol:** $V_s \to$ short, $I_s \to$ open *before* computing $R_{Th}$.
- ‚úÖ **Sign convention:** Inductor voltage in natural response is **opposite** to assumed current direction (Lenz's law).
- ‚úÖ For higher-order (second-order and beyond) source-free networks, the ODE becomes second-order and the solution involves **damped sinusoids** or **exponential modes** ‚ÄĒ covered in subsequent modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transient Response of R-L Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT412 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transient Response of R-L Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ‚ö° Module 3 ‚ÄĒ Transient Response of R-L Circuits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEICT412 - Module 3):**
> The **Transient Response** of an R-L circuit describes the behaviour of current and voltages in a resistor-inductor network during the period immediately after a switching event, before the circuit settles into its new **steady-state (forced) condition**. It is governed by the energy-storage property of the inductor, which opposes any sudden change in current.

### ūüĒĎ What is a "Transient"?
A **transient** is a temporary, short-lived response that occurs when a circuit transitions from one steady-state to another. In an R-L circuit, the inductor stores energy in its magnetic field, and the rate at which this energy is exchanged produces a brief, exponential settling behaviour.

### ūüĆä Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Think of a **heavy flywheel connected to a friction paddle** immersed in water:
- The **flywheel** is the **inductor (L)** ‚ÄĒ it resists sudden changes in speed (current).
- The **water resistance** is the **resistor (R)** ‚ÄĒ it dissipates kinetic energy.
- When you suddenly apply torque (a DC voltage), the wheel doesn't jump to full speed ‚ÄĒ it ramps up **smoothly and exponentially**.
- When you cut the torque, the wheel keeps spinning and gradually slows down to zero ‚ÄĒ again, **exponentially**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Golden Rules of R-L Transients (Board Favourite):**
> 1. The current through an inductor **cannot change instantaneously** (i(t‚āÄ‚ĀĽ) = i(t‚āÄ‚Āļ)).
> 2. The voltage across an inductor **CAN change instantaneously** (a jump is allowed).
> 3. The time constant is **ŌĄ = L/R** (in seconds).

### ūüďź Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Inductance (L):** measured in **Henry (H)**
- **Time constant (ŌĄ):** measured in **seconds (s)**
- **Initial Current (I‚āÄ):** determined by the circuit *just before* switching.
- **Final Current (I‚ąě):** determined by the circuit *a long time after* switching.

### 1.1 Types of Excitation in R-L Transients
| Excitation Type | Description | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Step Excitation** | A constant voltage/current is suddenly applied at t = 0. | ‚≠ź‚≠ź‚≠ź‚≠ź‚≠ź (Most Asked) |
| **Source-Free (Natural) Response** | A charged R-L circuit is disconnected from the source. | ‚≠ź‚≠ź‚≠ź‚≠ź |
| **Sinusoidal Excitation** | AC source switched in at t = 0 (covered in higher modules). | ‚≠ź‚≠ź |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Differential Equation

Consider a **series R-L circuit** connected to a DC source **V_s** through a switch closed at **t = 0**.

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop:

$$v_R(t) + v_L(t) = V_s$$

Substituting the **constitutive relations** $v_R = Ri$ and $v_L = L\dfrac{di}{dt}$:

$$L\frac{di(t)}{dt} + R\,i(t) = V_s$$

This is a **first-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)** with constant coefficients. Its complete solution has two parts:

$$i(t) = \underbrace{i_{forced}(t)}_{\text{Steady-state}} + \underbrace{i_{natural}(t)}_{\text{Transient}}$$

### 2.2 Solution Methodology ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Forced Response (Steady-State, $i_{ss}$):** At $t \to \infty$, the inductor behaves as a **short circuit** (di/dt = 0). Hence $i_{ss} = V_s / R$.
- **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Natural Response (Transient):** Solve the homogeneous equation $L\dfrac{di}{dt} + Ri = 0$, yielding $i_n = A e^{-t/\tau}$ where $\tau = L/R$.
- **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply Initial Condition:** $i(0^+) = i(0^-) = I_0$ (continuity of inductor current). Solve for $A$.
- **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Assemble the Total Solution.**

## 2.3 ūüďä KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Expression | Boundary Value at t = 0‚Āļ | Boundary Value at t ‚Üí ‚ąě |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time Constant | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | ‚ÄĒ | ‚ÄĒ |
| 2 | Forced (Steady) Current | $i_{ss} = \dfrac{V_s}{R}$ | ‚ÄĒ | $V_s / R$ |
| 3 | Total Current (Step Input) | $i(t) = \dfrac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + I_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ | $I_0$ | $V_s / R$ |
| 4 | Resistor Voltage | $v_R(t) = R\,i(t)$ | $R I_0$ | $V_s$ |
| 5 | Inductor Voltage | $v_L(t) = L\dfrac{di}{dt} = (V_s - R I_0)\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | $V_s - R I_0$ | $0$ |
| 6 | Energy Stored in L (max) | $W_L = \dfrac{1}{2}L I^2$ | $\frac{1}{2}L I_0^2$ | $\frac{1}{2}L I_{ss}^2$ |
| 7 | Source-Free Current | $i(t) = I_0 e^{-Rt/L}$ | $I_0$ | $0$ |
| 8 | Time to reach x% of $I_{ss}$ | $t_x = -\tau \ln(1 - x/100)$ | ‚ÄĒ | ‚ÄĒ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Transient Formula (KTU Examiner's Shortcut):**
> For *any* first-order circuit, the response of any quantity $x(t)$ is:
> $$x(t) = x(\infty) + \big[x(0^+) - x(\infty)\big]e^{-t/\tau}$$
> Memorise this ‚ÄĒ it works for **all** R-L and R-C problems on the exam.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility
- **Relay & Solenoid Driver Design:** Predicting the time taken for a coil to reach actuation current.
- **Switching Power Supplies:** Managing inrush currents through inductors.
- **Motor Starting:** Analysing armature current build-up in DC motors.
- **Spark Generation in Ignition Coils:** High induced voltage $v_L = -L\,di/dt$ when current is suddenly interrupted.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Step Response of a Series R-L Circuit (DC Excitation)

**Problem Setup:** A DC source $V_s$ is connected to a series R-L circuit at $t = 0$. The initial current is $i(0^-) = I_0$.

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Write the KVL Equation
$$L\frac{di}{dt} + R\,i = V_s \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Find the Steady-State (Particular) Solution
In steady state, $\frac{di}{dt} = 0$, so the inductor acts as a short circuit:

$$0 + R\,i_{ss} = V_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_{ss} = \frac{V_s}{R}$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Find the Transient (Homogeneous) Solution
Set the right-hand side to zero:

$$L\frac{di_n}{dt} + R\,i_n = 0$$

Rearrange into separable form:

$$\frac{di_n}{i_n} = -\frac{R}{L}\,dt$$

Integrate both sides:

$$\ln(i_n) = -\frac{R}{L}t + C$$

Exponentiate:

$$i_n = A\,e^{-Rt/L} = A\,e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \text{where } \tau = \frac{L}{R}$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Combine the Two Solutions
$$i(t) = i_{ss} + i_n = \frac{V_s}{R} + A\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Initial Condition $i(0^+) = I_0$
$$I_0 = \frac{V_s}{R} + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = I_0 - \frac{V_s}{R}$$

### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Write the Final Closed-Form Expression
$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R} + \left(I_0 - \frac{V_s}{R}\right)e^{-t/\tau}\,}$$

**Special Case ‚ÄĒ Zero Initial Current** ($I_0 = 0$):

$$i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Derive $v_R(t)$ and $v_L(t)$
Using Ohm's law:

$$v_R(t) = R\,i(t) = V_s + R\left(I_0 - \frac{V_s}{R}\right)e^{-t/\tau}$$

Simplify:

$$\boxed{\,v_R(t) = V_s + (R I_0 - V_s)\,e^{-t/\tau}\,}$$

Differentiate $i(t)$ to get $v_L(t)$:

$$v_L(t) = L\frac{di}{dt} = L \cdot \left(I_0 - \frac{V_s}{R}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{\tau}\right)e^{-t/\tau}$$

Since $\tau = L/R$, we have $L/\tau = R$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,v_L(t) = (V_s - R I_0)\,e^{-t/\tau}\,}$$

**Verification at Boundaries:**
- At $t = 0^+$: $v_R(0^+) = R I_0$ and $v_L(0^+) = V_s - R I_0$, and $v_R + v_L = V_s$ ‚úď
- At $t \to \infty$: $v_R(\infty) = V_s$ and $v_L(\infty) = 0$ ‚úď

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Source-Free (Natural) Response of R-L Circuit

**Problem Setup:** An R-L circuit carries current $I_0$ at $t = 0^-$ when the source is suddenly removed and the circuit is shorted through R (source-free / natural response).

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ KVL in the Closed Loop
With no source:

$$L\frac{di}{dt} + R\,i = 0$$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Solve the Homogeneous Equation
The general solution is:

$$i(t) = A\,e^{-Rt/L}$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Initial Condition
By the continuity of inductor current:

$$i(0^+) = i(0^-) = I_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = I_0$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Final Expression

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = I_0\,e^{-Rt/L} = I_0\,e^{-t/\tau}\,}$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Voltages
$$v_R(t) = R\,I_0\,e^{-t/\tau}$$
$$v_L(t) = L\frac{di}{dt} = -R\,I_0\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** on $v_L$ confirms the inductor is *delivering* its stored magnetic energy back to the circuit.

### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Energy Analysis
Total energy initially stored in the inductor:

$$W_L(0) = \frac{1}{2}L\,I_0^2$$

Energy dissipated in R over $[0, \infty)$:

$$W_R = \int_0^{\infty} i^2(t)\,R\,dt = R\,I_0^2 \int_0^{\infty} e^{-2Rt/L}\,dt = R\,I_0^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2R} = \frac{1}{2}L\,I_0^2$$

‚úÖ **Energy is conserved** ‚ÄĒ all the energy stored in L is dissipated in R, with no loss.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A series R-L circuit has $R = 10\,\Omega$ and $L = 2\,\text{H}$. It is connected to a $V_s = 100\,\text{V}$ DC supply at $t = 0$. Find:
(a) The time constant $\tau$.
(b) The current $i(t)$ assuming $i(0^-) = 0$.
(c) The time $t_1$ at which $i(t_1) = 50\,\text{A}$.
(d) The voltages $v_R(t)$ and $v_L(t)$.

### Solution
**Part (a):**
$$\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2\;\text{s}$$

**Part (b):** Using the formula with $I_0 = 0$:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = \frac{100}{10}\left(1 - e^{-t/0.2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = 10\left(1 - e^{-5t}\right)\;\text{A}\,}$$

**Part (c):** Setting $i(t_1) = 50\,\text{A}$:

$$50 = 10\left(1 - e^{-5t_1}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5 = 1 - e^{-5t_1}$$

$$e^{-5t_1} = -4$$

‚ö†ÔłŹ **No real solution** ‚ÄĒ the current can never reach 50 A! The maximum is 10 A. This illustrates a common KTU trick question to test conceptual understanding.

**Part (d):**
$$v_R(t) = R\,i(t) = 10 \cdot 10\left(1 - e^{-5t}\right) = 100\left(1 - e^{-5t}\right)\;\text{V}$$
$$v_L(t) = L\frac{di}{dt} = 2 \cdot 10 \cdot 5\,e^{-5t} = 100\,e^{-5t}\;\text{V}$$

**Verification:** $v_R(0^+) = 0$, $v_L(0^+) = 100\,\text{V}$ ‚úď; $v_R(\infty) = 100\,\text{V}$, $v_L(\infty) = 0$ ‚úď.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Schematic ‚ÄĒ Series R-L with DC Source

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vsrc[DC Source Vs]:::src
    SW[Switch closes at t=0]:::switch
    Rnode[Resistor R ohms]:::res
    Lnode[Inductor L henries]:::ind
    LoopA((Node A)):::node
    LoopB((Node B)):::node

    Vsrc --- SW
    SW --- LoopA
    LoopA --- Rnode
    Rnode --- Lnode
    Lnode --- LoopB
    LoopB --- Vsrc

    classDef src fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#c7d2fe,stroke:#4338ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ind fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚ÄĒ Transient Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Input: R, L, Vs, I0]:::input --> B[Step 1: Compute Time Constant tau = L/R]:::step
    B --> C[Step 2: Determine Initial Condition i 0+ = i 0-]:::step
    C --> D[Step 3: Determine Final Value i inf = Vs/R]:::step
    D --> E[Step 4: Apply Universal Transient Formula]:::step
    E --> F[Step 5: Differentiate for vL t]:::step
    F --> G[Step 6: Multiply by R for vR t]:::step
    G --> H[Step 7: Verify KVL vR + vL = Vs]:::verify
    H --> I[Final Response: i t, vR t, vL t]:::output

    classDef input fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef verify fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Waveform Sketch ‚ÄĒ Step Response of Series R-L Circuit

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TimeDomain[Response vs Time t in seconds]
        direction TB
        W1[i t rises from 0 to Vs/R]:::wave1
        W2[vR t rises from 0 to Vs]:::wave2
        W3[vL t decays from Vs to 0]:::wave3
    end
    W1 --- W2
    W2 --- W3

    classDef wave1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wave2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wave3 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation of the Waveforms:**
> - At $t = 0^+$: Current is at its **initial value**, inductor takes the **full source voltage**, resistor has the **leftover** voltage.
> - At $t = 5\tau$: The circuit has effectively reached steady state (within **99.3%** accuracy).
> - At all times: $v_R(t) + v_L(t) = V_s$ (KVL is always satisfied).

## 4.4 Source-Free Decay Waveform

```mermaid
graph LR
    SF0[t=0]:::t0 --> SF1[i=Io]:::pt
    SF1 --> SF2[tau: i=0.368 Io]:::pt
    SF2 --> SF3[2tau: i=0.135 Io]:::pt
    SF3 --> SF4[3tau: i=0.050 Io]:::pt
    SF4 --> SF5[5tau: approx 0]:::pt

    classDef t0 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pt fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ūüďė PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the time constant of an R-L circuit. What is its physical significance? What is the value of current at $t = \tau$ when initial current is zero?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The time constant $\tau$ of an R-L circuit is the time required for the current to reach **63.2%** of its final (steady-state) value, given by $\tau = L/R$.
- **Physical Significance (1 mark):** It characterises the *speed* of the transient response. A small $\tau$ means a fast response; a large $\tau$ means a slow response. It also represents the time at which the transient component decays to **36.8%** of its initial value.
- **Numerical Value (1 mark):** For $I_0 = 0$ and $i(\infty) = V_s/R$:

$$i(\tau) = \frac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = 0.632\,\frac{V_s}{R}$$

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the two initial conditions used to solve the transient response of a series R-L circuit excited by a DC source at $t = 0$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Current Continuity (2 marks):** The current through the inductor **cannot change instantaneously**. Therefore $i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-)$. This is determined from the circuit's steady state *just before* switching.
2. **Voltage Initial Value (1 mark):** The voltage across the inductor at $t = 0^+$ is obtained from KVL: $v_L(0^+) = V_s - R \cdot i_L(0^+)$. It *can* be discontinuous and is often the **full source voltage** if the inductor was un-energised before switching.

---

## ūüďē PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ‚Ěď Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ *[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the current $i(t)$ in a series R-L circuit when a DC voltage $V_s$ is suddenly applied at $t = 0$. Assume an initial current of $I_0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series R-L circuit with $R = 5\,\Omega$ and $L = 0.5\,\text{H}$ has an initial current of $I_0 = 4\,\text{A}$ (flowing in the direction of the applied source). At $t = 0$, a DC source of $V_s = 50\,\text{V}$ is connected. Find:
  (i) The total current $i(t)$ for $t > 0$.
  (ii) The voltages $v_R(t)$ and $v_L(t)$.
  (iii) The time required for the current to become **20 A**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Question A

### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Apply KVL for $t \geq 0$ **(1 mark)**:

$$L\frac{di}{dt} + R\,i = V_s$$

**Step 2:** Forced (steady-state) response ‚ÄĒ at $t \to \infty$, inductor ‚Üí short circuit **(1 mark)**:

$$i_{ss} = \frac{V_s}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Natural (transient) response ‚ÄĒ solve homogeneous equation **(1 mark)**:

$$\frac{di_n}{i_n} = -\frac{R}{L}dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; i_n = A\,e^{-Rt/L}$$

**Step 4:** Combine and apply initial condition $i(0^+) = I_0$ **(1 mark)**:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R} + A\,e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \tau = \frac{L}{R}$$

$$A = I_0 - \frac{V_s}{R}$$

**Step 5:** Final expression **(1 mark)**:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R} + \left(I_0 - \frac{V_s}{R}\right)e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 6:** Special case when $I_0 = 0$ and derivation of $v_R$, $v_L$ **(2 marks)**:

$$v_R(t) = V_s\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad v_L(t) = V_s\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

---

### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $R = 5\,\Omega$, $L = 0.5\,\text{H}$, $V_s = 50\,\text{V}$, $I_0 = 4\,\text{A}$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Time constant (1 mark):**

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{0.5}{5} = 0.1\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Steady-state current (1 mark):**

$$i_{ss} = \frac{V_s}{R} = \frac{50}{5} = 10\;\text{A}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Total current (1 mark):**

$$i(t) = 10 + (4 - 10)\,e^{-t/0.1} = 10 - 6\,e^{-10t}\;\text{A}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Voltages (2 marks):**

$$v_R(t) = R\,i(t) = 5\left(10 - 6e^{-10t}\right) = 50 - 30\,e^{-10t}\;\text{V}$$

$$v_L(t) = V_s - v_R(t) = 50 - (50 - 30e^{-10t}) = 30\,e^{-10t}\;\text{V}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Time to reach 20 A (2 marks):**

Setting $i(t_1) = 20\,\text{A}$:

$$20 = 10 - 6\,e^{-10t_1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -10 = -6\,e^{-10t_1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-10t_1} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.667$$

Since $e^{-x} \leq 1$ for $x \geq 0$, **no real solution exists**. The current monotonically rises from 4 A toward 10 A and can never reach 20 A.

> [!WARNING]
> **‚ö†ÔłŹ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> Many students blindly substitute $I_0 = 0$ in part (b) and assume a "fresh" circuit. **Read carefully** ‚ÄĒ the problem states the inductor initially carries 4 A in the *same direction* as the source. Failing to account for the non-zero initial current will cost you **3 full marks** out of 7 in part (b). Also, in part (b)(iii), do *not* compute a logarithm for an impossible value ‚ÄĒ write "no solution" and justify it.

---

### ‚Ěď Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ *[KTU University Exam - June 2023]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **source-free response** of an R-L circuit. Derive the expression for current and voltage, and show that all the energy initially stored in the inductor is dissipated in the resistor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An R-L circuit has $R = 4\,\Omega$ and $L = 2\,\text{H}$. The inductor carries an initial current of $I_0 = 6\,\text{A}$ at $t = 0^-$ when the source is removed (source-free condition). Find:
  (i) The current $i(t)$ and the inductor voltage $v_L(t)$.
  (ii) The total energy initially stored in L and the total energy dissipated in R.
  (iii) The time at which the current has decayed to **2% of its initial value**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Question B

### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Source-Free Response Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Circuit & KVL (2 marks):** When the source is disconnected and the L-R combination forms a closed loop, KVL gives:

$$L\frac{di}{dt} + R\,i = 0$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Solving the ODE (2 marks):** Integrating:

$$\int_{I_0}^{i(t)} \frac{di}{i} = -\frac{R}{L}\int_0^t dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln\frac{i(t)}{I_0} = -\frac{R}{L}t$$

$$i(t) = I_0\,e^{-Rt/L} = I_0\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Voltage and Energy (3 marks):**

$$v_L(t) = L\frac{di}{dt} = -R I_0 e^{-t/\tau}, \quad v_R(t) = R I_0 e^{-t/\tau}$$

Initial energy stored:

$$W_L(0) = \frac{1}{2}L I_0^2$$

Energy dissipated in R:

$$W_R = \int_0^{\infty} v_R(t) \cdot i(t)\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} R I_0^2 e^{-2Rt/L}\,dt = \frac{1}{2}L I_0^2$$

Hence **$W_R = W_L(0)$** ‚ÄĒ the total energy is conserved. ‚úď

---

### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $R = 4\,\Omega$, $L = 2\,\text{H}$, $I_0 = 6\,\text{A}$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Time constant (1 mark):**

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Current and Inductor Voltage (2 marks):**

$$i(t) = 6\,e^{-t/0.5} = 6\,e^{-2t}\;\text{A}$$

$$v_L(t) = -R I_0\,e^{-2t} = -4 \cdot 6 \cdot e^{-2t} = -24\,e^{-2t}\;\text{V}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Initial and Dissipated Energy (2 marks):**

$$W_L(0) = \frac{1}{2}(2)(6)^2 = 36\;\text{J}$$

$$W_R = \frac{1}{2}L I_0^2 = 36\;\text{J} \quad (\text{all energy is dissipated in R})$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Time for 2% decay (2 marks):** We need $i(t_1) = 0.02 \times 6 = 0.12\,\text{A}$:

$$0.12 = 6\,e^{-2t_1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-2t_1} = 0.02$$

$$-2t_1 = \ln(0.02) = -3.912 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_1 = 1.956\;\text{s}$$

Alternatively, using the rule that current decays to ~1% at $t = 5\tau = 2.5\,\text{s}$, our 2% time of $1.956\,\text{s}$ is consistent (between $3\tau$ and $4\tau$). ‚úď

> [!WARNING]
> **‚ö†ÔłŹ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Negative sign on $v_L$:** Many students drop the minus sign in $v_L(t) = -R I_0 e^{-t/\tau}$. The sign is **critical** ‚ÄĒ it indicates that the inductor is *acting as a source* and delivering energy. Losing this sign costs you **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Energy integration:** Do not write $W_R = 0$. The integration $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-2Rt/L} dt$ requires the factor of **2 in the exponent**. Forgetting this gives a *wrong answer of 72 J*. Practice this integration carefully.

---

## ūüĒĀ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for R-L Transients (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- ‚úÖ The **time constant** of an R-L circuit is $\tau = L/R$ ‚ÄĒ never $RC$.
- ‚úÖ The **inductor current is continuous** at $t = 0$; the **inductor voltage can jump**.
- ‚úÖ Use the **Universal Transient Formula**: $x(t) = x(\infty) + [x(0^+) - x(\infty)] e^{-t/\tau}$.
- ‚úÖ At $t = 0^+$: inductor acts as an **open circuit** (in the sense of holding current constant) but its *terminal voltage* is whatever KVL demands.
- ‚úÖ At $t \to \infty$: inductor acts as a **short circuit** ($v_L = 0$).
- ‚úÖ Energy conservation: in a **source-free** R-L circuit, all $W_L = \frac{1}{2}LI_0^2$ is dissipated in $R$.
- ‚úÖ The current reaches **63.2%** of $i(\infty)$ at $t = \tau$, **86.5%** at $t = 2\tau$, **95%** at $t = 3\tau$, **98.2%** at $t = 4\tau$, and **99.3%** at $t = 5\tau$ (‚Čą steady state).
- ‚úÖ Always **verify KVL** at $t = 0^+$ and $t \to \infty$ to catch algebraic sign errors.
- ‚úÖ The **DC steady-state** of an R-L circuit is just Ohm's law: $I = V/R$.
- ‚úÖ For the **source-free case**, $v_L$ has the **opposite polarity** of $v_R$ (inductor is the source).
- ‚úÖ Common KTU trick: ask for a time that is *physically impossible* (e.g., current exceeding $V_s/R$) ‚ÄĒ recognise it and state "no real solution" with justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R-L-C Circuits (Series and Parallel Combination) for Sinusoidal Excitations - Initial Conditions - Solution Method Using Laplace Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT412 - NETWORK THEORY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** R-L-C Circuits (Series and Parallel Combination) for Sinusoidal Excitations - Initial Conditions - Solution Method Using Laplace Transforms # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **R-L-C Circuit** is a second-order linear time-invariant (LTI) electrical network containing one resistor (R), one inductor (L), and one capacitor (C) connected either in **series** or **parallel** topology. When excited by a **sinusoidal source** $v_s(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ or $i_s(t) = I_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$, the circuit produces a response that consists of two mathematically distinct components: 1. **Transient Response (Natural / Complementary Response):** Governed solely by the energy-storage elements (L and C) and the dissipative element (R). It decays exponentially with time, dependent on the **initial conditions** $i_L(0^-)$ and $v_C(0^-)$. 2. **Steady-State Response (Forced / Particular Response):** Persists indefinitely after transients vanish. It oscillates at the **same frequency $\omega$** as the source (for sinusoidal excitation). The **complete response** is their algebraic sum: $$i(t) = i_{tr}(t) + i_{ss}(t)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Laplace Transform method is the *preferred* analytical tool for solving R-L-C circuits because it **automatically handles initial conditions** through equivalent source representations of L and C in the s-domain, avoiding the need for classical differential equation techniques. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of an R-L-C circuit as a **mechanical mass-spring-damper system**: | Mechanical Element | Electrical Equivalent | Energy Role | |---|---|---| | Mass ($m$) | Inductor ($L$) | Stores kinetic energy | | Spring ($k$) | Capacitor ($1/C$) | Stores potential energy | | Dashpot Damper ($b$) | Resistor ($R$) | Dissipates energy | When you push a child on a swing (apply sinusoidal force), the swing oscillates. If there's no friction (R = 0), the swing oscillates forever at its natural frequency. Add some friction (R > 0), and the oscillations **decay** until only the forced motion remains. The **initial push** given to the swing corresponds to the **initial conditions** in the electrical circuit. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The resistor R determines whether the response is *overdamped* (no oscillation, slow decay), *critically damped* (fastest non-oscillatory decay), or *underdamped* (decaying oscillation). This analogy helps predict circuit behavior without heavy math. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Neper Frequency (Damping Coefficient):** $\alpha$ ‚ÄĒ measured in **Np/s (Neper per second)** - **Resonant (Undamped Natural) Frequency:** $\omega_0$ ‚ÄĒ measured in **rad/s** - **Damped Natural Frequency:** $\omega_d$ ‚ÄĒ measured in **rad/s** - **Quality Factor:** $Q = \omega_0 / (2\alpha)$ ‚ÄĒ dimensionless - **Time Constant:** $\tau = 1/\alpha$ ‚ÄĒ measured in **seconds (s)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Series R-L-C Circuit ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).** For a series circuit driven by source $v_s(t)$: $$v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t) = v_s(t)$$ **Step 2: Substitute element equations.** $$R \cdot i(t) + L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}\int_{-\infty}^{t} i(\tau)\,d\tau = v_s(t)$$ **Step 3: Differentiate once to obtain a 2nd-order ODE** (eliminates the integral): $$L \frac{d^2 i(t)}{dt^2} + R \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} i(t) = \frac{dv_s(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 4: Why this matters?** The **left side** describes the circuit's intrinsic behavior. The **right side** is the forcing function. Solving requires the **characteristic equation** to find the natural response. ## 2.2 The Parallel R-L-C Circuit ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL).** For a parallel circuit driven by source $i_s(t)$: $$i_R(t) + i_L(t) + i_C(t) = i_s(t)$$ **Step 2: Substitute element equations** with $v(t)$ as the common branch voltage: $$\frac{v(t)}{R} + \frac{1}{L}\int_{-\infty}^{t} v(\tau)\,d\tau + C\frac{dv(t)}{dt} = i_s(t)$$ **Step 3: Differentiate once:** $$C \frac{d^2 v(t)}{dt^2} + \frac{1}{R} \frac{dv(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{L} v(t) = \frac{di_s(t)}{dt}$$ ## 2.3 Characteristic Equation and Root Classification The **characteristic equation** is obtained by substituting $i(t) = e^{st}$ (or $v(t) = e^{st}$) into the homogeneous part of the ODE. **For Series RLC:** $$L s^2 + R s + \frac{1}{C} = 0$$ **For Parallel RLC:** $$C s^2 + \frac{1}{R} s + \frac{1}{L} = 0$$ **Roots:** $$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$ **Damping Conditions (Crucial for KTU Problems):** | Condition | Inequality | Roots Nature | Response Shape | |---|---|---|---| | Overdamped | $\alpha > \omega_0$ | Two distinct real, negative | Sum of two decaying exponentials | | Critically Damped | $\alpha = \omega_0$ | Repeated real, negative $s = -\alpha$ | $(A_1 + A_2 t)e^{-\alpha t}$ | | Underdamped | $\alpha < \omega_0$ | Complex conjugates $-\alpha \pm j\omega_d$ | Decaying sinusoid $e^{-\alpha t}\cos(\omega_d t + \theta)$ | | Undamped | $\alpha = 0$ (R = 0) | Pure imaginary $\pm j\omega_0$ | Sustained sinusoid | **Definitions:** $$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} \text{ (series)} \quad \text{or} \quad \alpha = \frac{1}{2RC} \text{ (parallel)}$$ $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(same for both)}$$ $$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} \quad \text{(underdamped case)}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master this table ‚ÄĒ virtually every KTU Module 3 numerical problem depends on it. | Quantity | Series RLC | Parallel RLC | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Governing ODE | $L i'' + R i' + i/C = v_s'$ | $C v'' + v'/R + v/L = i_s'$ | ‚ÄĒ | | Characteristic Eq. | $L s^2 + R s + 1/C = 0$ | $C s^2 + s/R + 1/L = 0$ | ‚ÄĒ | | Damping $\alpha$ | $R/(2L)$ | $1/(2RC)$ | **Np/s** | | Natural Freq. $\omega_0$ | $1/\sqrt{LC}$ | $1/\sqrt{LC}$ | **rad/s** | | Damped Freq. $\omega_d$ | $\sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | $\sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | **rad/s** | | Quality Factor $Q$ | $\omega_0 L / R$ | $R / (\omega_0 L) = \omega_0 R C$ | dimensionless | | Inductor s-model | $sL$ in series with $L i_L(0^-)$ | $sL$ in parallel with $i_L(0^-)/s$ | ‚ÄĒ | | Capacitor s-model | $1/(sC)$ in series with $v_C(0^-)/s$ | $1/(sC)$ in parallel with $C v_C(0^-)$ | ‚ÄĒ | | Impedance $Z(s)$ | $R + sL + 1/(sC)$ | ‚ÄĒ | $\Omega$ | | Admittance $Y(s)$ | ‚ÄĒ | $1/R + 1/(sL) + sC$ | **S (Siemens)** | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Radio/TV Tuners:** Series/parallel RLC circuits select a station's carrier frequency by exploiting resonance at $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$. - **Automotive Ignition Systems:** Underdamped RLC circuits generate the high-voltage spark. - **Power System Filters:** Suppress harmonics in transmission lines. - **Oscillator Design:** Undamped LC circuits form the heart of sinusoidal oscillators. - **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** RLC transient analysis is critical for designing snubber circuits. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Series RLC Circuit Response Using Laplace Transform **Problem Setup:** A series RLC circuit with source $v_s(t)$, initial inductor current $i_L(0^-)$ and initial capacitor voltage $v_C(0^-)$. **Step 1: Convert each element to s-domain with initial conditions.** | Element | Time Domain | s-Domain Model | |---|---|---| | Resistor | $v_R = R i$ | $V_R(s) = R I(s)$ | | Inductor | $v_L = L\, di/dt$ | $V_L(s) = sL\, I(s) - L\, i_L(0^-)$ | | Capacitor | $i_C = C\, dv/dt$ | $V_C(s) = \frac{I(s)}{sC} + \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention:** The voltage source $L\, i_L(0^-)$ is in series with $sL$, with polarity such that it represents the initial current flowing into the inductor's positive terminal. The voltage source $v_C(0^-)/s$ is in series with $1/(sC)$, with polarity matching the assumed $v_C$ polarity. **Step 2: Write the s-domain KVL equation.** $$V_s(s) = R I(s) + sL I(s) - L i_L(0^-) + \frac{I(s)}{sC} + \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$ **Step 3: Group terms with $I(s)$.** $$V_s(s) + L i_L(0^-) - \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s} = \left[ R + sL + \frac{1}{sC} \right] I(s)$$ **Step 4: Recognize the impedance $Z(s)$.** $$Z(s) = R + sL + \frac{1}{sC} = \frac{L s^2 + R s + 1/C}{s}$$ **Step 5: Solve for $I(s)$.** $$I(s) = \frac{V_s(s) + L i_L(0^-) - v_C(0^-)/s}{Z(s)} = \frac{s \left[ V_s(s) + L i_L(0^-) \right] - v_C(0^-)}{L s^2 + R s + 1/C}$$ **Step 6: Apply partial fraction expansion and inverse Laplace transform** to get $i(t)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Parallel RLC Circuit Response Using Laplace Transform **Step 1: s-domain element models for parallel circuits.** | Element | s-Domain Model (Parallel Form) | |---|---| | Resistor | $I_R(s) = V(s)/R$ | | Inductor | $I_L(s) = \frac{V(s)}{sL} + \frac{i_L(0^-)}{s}$ | | Capacitor | $I_C(s) = sC\, V(s) - C v_C(0^-)$ | > [!NOTE] > **For parallel circuits:** The initial inductor current appears as a **current source** $i_L(0^-)/s$ in parallel, and the initial capacitor voltage appears as a **current source** $C v_C(0^-)$ in parallel. **Step 2: Write s-domain KCL at the top node.** $$I_s(s) = \frac{V(s)}{R} + \frac{V(s)}{sL} + \frac{i_L(0^-)}{s} + sC V(s) - C v_C(0^-)$$ **Step 3: Group terms with $V(s)$.** $$I_s(s) + C v_C(0^-) - \frac{i_L(0^-)}{s} = \left[ \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{sL} + sC \right] V(s)$$ **Step 4: Recognize admittance $Y(s)$.** $$Y(s) = \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{sL} + sC = \frac{C s^2 + s/R + 1/L}{s}$$ **Step 5: Solve for $V(s)$.** $$V(s) = \frac{I_s(s) + C v_C(0^-) - i_L(0^-)/s}{Y(s)} = \frac{s \left[ I_s(s) + C v_C(0^-) \right] - i_L(0^-)}{C s^2 + s/R + 1/L}$$ --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: Series RLC with Sinusoidal Excitation **Given:** - $R = 6\ \Omega$, $L = 1\ \text{H}$, $C = 1/5\ \text{F}$ - $v_s(t) = 10 \cos(2t)\ u(t)\ \text{V}$ - $i_L(0^-) = 0\ \text{A}$, $v_C(0^-) = 0\ \text{V}$ **Step 1: Find $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$.** $$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{6}{2(1)} = 3\ \text{Np/s}$$ $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 \cdot 1/5}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236\ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 2: Identify damping condition.** Since $\alpha = 3 > \omega_0 = \sqrt{5}$, the circuit is **overdamped**. **Step 3: Solve characteristic equation.** $$s^2 + 6s + 5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (s+1)(s+5) = 0$$ $$s_1 = -1, \quad s_2 = -5$$ **Step 4: Find $V_s(s)$.** $$V_s(s) = \mathcal{L}\{10\cos(2t)\} = \frac{10s}{s^2 + 4}$$ **Step 5: Compute $Z(s)$.** $$Z(s) = 6 + s + \frac{5}{s} = \frac{s^2 + 6s + 5}{s}$$ **Step 6: Compute $I(s)$ with zero initial conditions.** $$I(s) = \frac{V_s(s)}{Z(s)} = \frac{10s}{s^2+4} \cdot \frac{s}{s^2 + 6s + 5} = \frac{10 s^2}{(s^2+4)(s+1)(s+5)}$$ **Step 7: Partial fraction expansion.** $$\frac{10 s^2}{(s^2+4)(s+1)(s+5)} = \frac{As + B}{s^2 + 4} + \frac{C}{s+1} + \frac{D}{s+5}$$ **Solve for C (multiply by $s+1$, set $s = -1$):** $$C = \frac{10(-1)^2}{((-1)^2 + 4)(-1+5)} = \frac{10}{5 \cdot 4} = 0.5$$ **Solve for D (multiply by $s+5$, set $s = -5$):** $$D = \frac{10(-5)^2}{((-5)^2+4)(-5+1)} = \frac{250}{29 \cdot (-4)} = -\frac{250}{116} = -\frac{125}{58} \approx -2.155$$ **Solve for A and B (cross-multiply and equate coefficients):** $$10 s^2 = (As+B)(s+1)(s+5) + C(s^2+4)(s+5) + D(s^2+4)(s+1)$$ Expanding systematically: - $(As+B)(s+1)(s+5) = (As+B)(s^2 + 6s + 5) = A s^3 + 6A s^2 + 5A s + B s^2 + 6B s + 5B$ - $C(s^2+4)(s+5) = C(s^3 + 5s^2 + 4s + 20) = C s^3 + 5C s^2 + 4C s + 20C$ - $D(s^2+4)(s+1) = D(s^3 + s^2 + 4s + 4) = D s^3 + D s^2 + 4D s + 4D$ Summing coefficients of $s^3$: $$A + C + D = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -C - D = -0.5 - (-125/58)$$ $$A = -0.5 + 125/58 = -29/58 + 125/58 = 96/58 = 48/29 \approx 1.655$$ Summing coefficients of $s^2$: $$6A + 5A_{\text{from }B} + 5C + D = 10$$ $$6A + B + 5C + D = 10$$ $$B = 10 - 6A - 5C - D = 10 - 6(48/29) - 5(0.5) - (-125/58)$$ $$B = 10 - 288/29 - 2.5 + 125/58$$ $$B = 7.5 - 288/29 + 125/58$$ $$B = 7.5 - 576/58 + 125/58 = 7.5 - 451/58$$ $$B = 435/58 - 451/58 = -16/58 = -8/29 \approx -0.276$$ **Step 8: Apply inverse Laplace transform.** $$i(t) = \left[ \frac{48}{29}\cos(2t) - \frac{8}{29}\sin(2t) \right] u(t) + 0.5\, e^{-t} u(t) - \frac{125}{58} e^{-5t} u(t)$$ **Step 9: Simplify the steady-state sinusoid into single cosine.** $$\text{Amplitude} = \sqrt{(48/29)^2 + (8/29)^2} = \frac{\sqrt{2304 + 64}}{29} = \frac{\sqrt{2368}}{29} = \frac{8\sqrt{37}}{29}$$ $$\text{Phase } \phi = \arctan\left( \frac{-8/29}{48/29} \right) = \arctan(-1/6) \approx -9.46^\circ$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{i(t) = \frac{8\sqrt{37}}{29} \cos(2t - 9.46^\circ) + 0.5\, e^{-t} - 2.155\, e^{-5t} \quad \text{A},\ t \ge 0}$$ **Verification:** At $t=0$: $i(0) = (8\sqrt{37}/29)\cos(-9.46^\circ) + 0.5 - 2.155 \approx 1.655 - 1.655 = 0\ \text{A}$ ‚úď (matches zero initial current). --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Initial Condition Source Models (Summary Table) | Initial Condition | Series RLC s-Domain Insertion | Parallel RLC s-Domain Insertion | |---|---|---| | $i_L(0^-) \neq 0$ | Voltage source $L i_L(0^-)$ in **series** with $sL$ | Current source $i_L(0^-)/s$ in **parallel** with $sL$ | | $v_C(0^-) \neq 0$ | Voltage source $v_C(0^-)/s$ in **series** with $1/(sC)$ | Current source $C v_C(0^-)$ in **parallel** with $1/(sC)$ | **Memorization Tip:** Inductors transform current initial conditions into voltage sources; capacitors transform voltage initial conditions into voltage sources (in series form) or current sources (in parallel form). # 4.
