```mermaid
    A[Filter Order Selection] --> B[First Order Filter]
    A --> C[Second Order Filter]
    A --> D[Higher Order Filter]
    B --> E[Roll-off minus 20 dB per decade]
    C --> F[Roll-off minus 40 dB per decade]
    D --> G[Roll-off minus N times 20 dB per decade]
    E --> H[Single Energy Storage Element R or C]
    F --> I[Two Energy Storage Elements L and C]
    G --> J[Cascaded Filter Sections for Sharper Response]
    H --> K[Used in Audio Tone Control]
    I --> L[Used in RF Communication Systems]
    J --> M[Used in Precision Instrumentation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of filter selection, response curves, and order-based roll-off behavior. The test points ($\omega = 0$, $\omega = \omega_0$, $\omega = \infty$) form the **Three-Point Identification Method** ‚ÄĒ the most reliable way to identify filter types in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Low Pass Filter. State its cutoff frequency equation for an RC configuration.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is a frequency-selective two-port network that passes all frequencies from **0 Hz (DC) to a specified cutoff frequency $f_c$** with minimal attenuation, and rejects (attenuates) all frequencies above $f_c$.

For a simple **RC low pass filter** with output taken across the capacitor, the cutoff frequency is given by:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

where $R$ is in ohms ($\Omega$) and $C$ is in farads (F). The transfer function is:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often confuse LPF with HPF. Remember: the **output across the capacitor gives LPF**; the **output across the resistor gives HPF**.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Band Pass and Band Elimination filters based on their magnitude response.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Band Pass Filter (BPF) | Band Elimination Filter (BEF) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Passband | One specific band $f_1$ to $f_2$ | Two bands: $0$ to $f_1$ AND $f_2$ to $\infty$ |
| Stopband | $0$ to $f_1$ AND $f_2$ to $\infty$ | One specific band $f_1$ to $f_2$ |
| Magnitude at $f_0$ | **Maximum (equals 1)** | **Minimum (equals 0)** ‚ÄĒ notch |
| Application | Radio tuning, channel selection | 50/60 Hz hum removal, anti-aliasing |
| Common name | Mid-band selector | Notch filter / Band-stop filter |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For the circuit shown below, derive the transfer function $H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ and identify the type of filter it represents. Given: $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $L = 10\text{ mH}$, $C = 1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the resonant frequency, bandwidth, and quality factor for the same circuit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The circuit is a series RLC with output taken across the resistor $R$ (Band Pass configuration).

Total impedance: $Z_{total} = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$

Applying voltage divider:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R}{R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC + (j\omega)^2 LC}$$

This is the **standard form of a Band Pass Filter** ‚ÄĒ confirmed because:
- At $\omega = 0$: $H(j0) = 0$ (DC blocked)
- At $\omega = \infty$: $H(j\infty) = 0$ (high freq blocked)
- At $\omega = \omega_0$: $H(j\omega_0) = 1$ (band passes)

**Part (b):**

Resonant frequency:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10^{-8}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz}$$

Bandwidth:

$$BW = \frac{R}{2\pi L} = \frac{1000}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{0.0628}$$

$$BW \approx 15915.5 \text{ Hz}$$

Quality Factor:

$$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{1591.55}{15915.5} \approx 0.1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently make errors in the bandwidth formula. Remember: $BW = \frac{R}{2\pi L}$ **only for series RLC** band pass filters. For parallel RLC configurations, the formula becomes $BW = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss!

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Design a first-order RC Low Pass Filter with a cutoff frequency of **10 kHz**. Choose $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and calculate the required value of $R$. Also draw the magnitude response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same LPF, calculate the magnitude of $H(j\omega)$ in dB at frequencies **1 kHz, 10 kHz, and 100 kHz**. Comment on the roll-off rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Given: $f_c = 10\text{ kHz}$, $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\text{ F}$.

Using the cutoff frequency formula:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi}$$

$$R \approx 1591.55 \text{ }\Omega \approx 1.59 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Circuit:** Series $R = 1.59\text{ k}\Omega$ with output taken across $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

**Part (b):**

The transfer function magnitude is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

With $RC = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c} = 1.5915 \times 10^{-5}$ seconds.

**At $f = 1$ kHz:**

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi \times 10^3 \times 1.5915 \times 10^{-5})^2}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0.1^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.01}} \approx 0.995$$

In dB: $20\log_{10}(0.995) \approx -0.043$ dB

**At $f = 10$ kHz (cutoff):**

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 1^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$$

In dB: $20\log_{10}(0.7071) = -3$ dB (as expected for cutoff)

**At $f = 100$ kHz:**

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 10^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{101}} \approx 0.0995$$

In dB: $20\log_{10}(0.0995) \approx -20.04$ dB

**Roll-off Analysis:** From 10 kHz to 100 kHz (one decade), the gain dropped from ‚ąí3 dB to ‚ąí20 dB, a change of **‚ąí17 dB per decade**. This is slightly less than the theoretical **‚ąí20 dB/decade** because the magnitude at 10 kHz is already ‚ąí3 dB. The asymptotic roll-off is **‚ąí20 dB/decade**, confirming this is a **first-order filter**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common mistake in such design problems is **forgetting to convert units** properly. If $C$ is given in $\mu\text{F}$ and $R$ is expected in $k\Omega$, you must convert to base units (F and $\Omega$) before applying the formula. Additionally, **do not round off intermediate values** ‚ÄĒ this compounds errors in subsequent calculations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ‚ÄĒ Filters Module**

### **Core Concepts:**
- ‚ėź A **filter** is a frequency-selective network that passes a desired band and rejects others.
- ‚ėź **Four canonical types**: LPF, HPF, BPF, BEF.
- ‚ėź **Cutoff frequency** = the ‚ąí3 dB point where power drops to half and voltage to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.
- ‚ėź **Order of filter** = highest power of $s = j\omega$ in the denominator of $H(s)$.
- ‚ėź **Roll-off rate** = **‚ąí20 √ó N dB/decade**, where N is the filter order.

### **Identification Rules (Three-Point Test):**
- ‚ėź **LPF**: $|H(0)| = 1$ AND $|H(\infty)| = 0$ (passes DC, blocks high freq)
- ‚ėź **HPF**: $|H(0)| = 0$ AND $|H(\infty)| = 1$ (blocks DC, passes high freq)
- ‚ėź **BPF**: $|H(0)| = 0$, $|H(\omega_0)| = 1$, $|H(\infty)| = 0$ (passes only middle band)
- ‚ėź **BEF**: $|H(0)| = 1$, $|H(\omega_0)| = 0$, $|H(\infty)| = 1$ (blocks only middle band)

### **Key Formulas (Memorize!):**
- ‚ėź LPF/HPF cutoff: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$
- ‚ėź Resonant frequency: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$
- ‚ėź BPF/BEF bandwidth: $BW = \frac{R}{2\pi L}$ (series) or $\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (parallel)
- ‚ėź Quality factor: $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$
- ‚ėź Transfer functions of all four types (see Formula Sheet in Section 2.6)

### **Common Pitfalls:**
- ‚ėź Confusing series and parallel RLC formulas for bandwidth.
- ‚ėź Forgetting to convert $C$ from $\mu\text{F}$ to F, or $f$ from kHz to Hz.
- ‚ėź Mixing up LPF (output across C) with HPF (output across R) in RC circuits.
- ‚ėź Reporting magnitude in dB without using the $20\log_{10}$ formula.
- ‚ėź Not specifying units in final answers (always include Hz, $\Omega$, F, etc.).

### **Engineering Applications to Remember:**
- ‚ėź **LPF**: Audio bass boost, ripple filters in power supplies, anti-aliasing.
- ‚ėź **HPF**: AC coupling, DC blocking, high-frequency emphasis in audio.
- ‚ėź **BPF**: Radio receivers, channel selection, spectrum analyzers.
- ‚ėź **BEF**: 50/60 Hz notch filters for biomedical signals, DSL splitters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filter Design and Implementation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT412 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Filter Design and Implementation

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electric Filter?

An **electric filter** is a frequency-selective two-port network designed to transmit (pass) sinusoidal input signals within a specified range of frequencies while attenuating (rejecting) signals of other frequencies. The frequency range that is passed with minimal attenuation is called the **passband**, and the range rejected is the **stopband**. The boundary separating them is the **cutoff frequency** $\omega_c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A filter is a reactive network (containing inductors and capacitors) or an active network (containing op-amps with RC components) whose transfer function $H(j\omega)$ has a magnitude response that meets prescribed specifications on gain, passband ripple, stopband attenuation, and transition bandwidth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The Sieve of Frequencies

Imagine a **kitchen sieve**. When you pour a mixture of large stones and fine sand, the sand falls through (passed) while the stones are retained (rejected). An electric filter works exactly the same way on signals:

- A **low-pass filter (LPF)** is like a sieve that lets only slow, large grains (low frequencies) through and holds back small, fast particles (high frequencies).
- A **high-pass filter (HPF)** does the opposite ‚ÄĒ it lets the fast, fine particles through.
- A **band-pass filter (BPF)** is like a window screen letting a specific size of particles through while blocking both larger and smaller ones.
- A **band-stop (notch) filter (BSF)** is a sieve that blocks only one specific size and lets everything else through.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Filters? ‚ÄĒ Engineering Utility

| Application | Required Filter |
|-------------|-----------------|
| Audio equalizer (bass/treble control) | Low-pass and High-pass |
| Radio receiver (selecting station) | Band-pass |
| Removing 50 Hz power-line hum from biomedical signals | Notch (Band-stop) |
| Anti-aliasing before ADC | Low-pass |
| DC blocking in amplifiers | High-pass |
| Image rejection in communication | Band-pass |

## 1.4 Classification of Filters (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Filters can be broadly classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis 1 ‚ÄĒ By Frequency Response Type:**
> 1. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** ‚ÄĒ passes $0 \le \omega \le \omega_c$
> 2. **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** ‚ÄĒ passes $\omega_c \le \omega \le \infty$
> 3. **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** ‚ÄĒ passes $\omega_{c1} \le \omega \le \omega_{c2}$
> 4. **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** ‚ÄĒ rejects $\omega_{c1} \le \omega \le \omega_{c2}$
>
> **Axis 2 ‚ÄĒ By Active / Passive Elements:**
> - **Passive filters:** built using only $R$, $L$, $C$ (no gain).
> - **Active filters:** built using $R$, $C$ and an active element like an op-amp (provides gain and isolation).
>
> **Axis 3 ‚ÄĒ By Approximation Function:**
> - **Butterworth** (maximally flat passband)
> - **Chebyshev** (ripple in passband, sharper cutoff)
> - **Bessel** (linear phase / constant group delay)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ideal vs. Practical Filter Magnitude Responses

The **ideal** brick-wall response is physically unrealizable (it is non-causal). Practical filters approximate this shape using rational transfer functions:

$$H(s) = \frac{b_m s^m + b_{m-1} s^{m-1} + \cdots + b_0}{a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0}$$

For a low-pass prototype, this becomes:

$$H(s) = \frac{K \cdot \omega_c^n}{s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0}$$

- The **order $n$** of the filter is the highest power of $s$ in the denominator. Higher $n$ means sharper roll-off (closer to ideal).
- The **roll-off rate** is **$20n$ dB/decade** beyond cutoff.
- The **transition band** is the gap between the passband edge $\omega_p$ and the stopband edge $\omega_s$.

## 2.2 The Butterworth Approximation ‚ÄĒ "Maximally Flat" Design

The Butterworth magnitude response is given by:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left( \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c} \right)^{2n}}}$$

**Why Butterworth?**
- At $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \omega_c$, the first $2n-1$ derivatives of $\vert H \vert$ are zero. This is why it is called **maximally flat** ‚ÄĒ there is no ripple in the passband.
- All-pole design (zeros only at infinity) ‚ÄĒ simple to realize.

> [!TIP]
> For $\omega \ll \omega_c$, the response is essentially flat with no loss. For $\omega \gg \omega_c$, the response rolls off at $-20n$ dB/decade.

## 2.3 Order of Butterworth Filter (Formula Sheet Highlight)

Given design specifications $A_p$ (max passband attenuation in dB at $\omega_p$) and $A_s$ (min stopband attenuation in dB at $\omega_s$):

$$n \ge \frac{\log_{10}\left( \sqrt{\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}} \right)}{\log_{10}\left( \dfrac{\omega_s}{\omega_p} \right)}$$

The poles of a Butterworth filter of order $n$ lie on a circle of radius $\omega_c$ in the $s$-plane, equally spaced by $\dfrac{\pi}{n}$ radians, symmetric about the real axis. Poles are located at:

$$s_k = \omega_c \, e^{\,j\left[\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

(Only poles in the **left half-plane** are selected for stability.)

## 2.4 Frequency Transformations (The Key Trick)

Rather than designing HP, BP, BS filters from scratch, we **start with a normalized low-pass prototype** ($\omega_c = 1$ rad/s) and substitute the variable $s$ with a transformation function. This is the standard KTU design approach.

| Desired Filter | Transformation | Mapping Formula |
|----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Low-Pass (LPF) | $s \to \dfrac{s}{\omega_c}$ | $\omega$ stays normalized, then scale |
| High-Pass (HPF) | $s \to \dfrac{\omega_c}{s}$ | Inverts frequency axis |
| Band-Pass (BPF) | $s \to \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{B \, s}$ | $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_1 \omega_2}$, $B = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ |
| Band-Stop (BSF) | $s \to \dfrac{B \, s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2}$ | Notch centered at $\omega_0$ |

## 2.5 Magnitude Scaling & Frequency Scaling

To convert a **normalized** filter ($R = 1\,\Omega$, $\omega_c = 1$ rad/s) to practical values:

- **Magnitude scaling** by factor $k_m$: $\quad R' = k_m R, \quad L' = k_m L, \quad C' = \dfrac{C}{k_m}$
- **Frequency scaling** by factor $k_f$: $\quad R' = R, \quad L' = \dfrac{L}{k_f}, \quad C' = \dfrac{C}{k_f}$

Combined: $R' = k_m R, \quad L' = \dfrac{k_m}{k_f} L, \quad C' = \dfrac{C}{k_m \, k_f}$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Butterworth magnitude | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^{2n}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Roll-off rate | $20n$ dB/decade | For $\omega \gg \omega_c$ |
| 3 | Filter order | $n \ge \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s}-1}{10^{0.1 A_p}-1}}\right)}{\log_{10}(\omega_s/\omega_p)}$ | $A_p, A_s$ in dB |
| 4 | BPF center frequency | $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_1 \omega_2}$ | rad/s |
| 5 | BPF bandwidth | $B = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Quality factor $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{B} = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Magnitude scaling | $L \to k_m L, \; C \to C/k_m$ | $k_m > 0$ |
| 8 | Frequency scaling | $L \to L/k_f, \; C \to C/k_f$ | $k_f > 0$ |
| 9 | Sallen-Key LPF natural freq. | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | rad/s |
| 10 | Sallen-Key damping factor | $\zeta = \dfrac{1}{2Q}$ (or $1/2Q$ for unity gain) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Chebyshev ripple factor | $\epsilon = \sqrt{10^{0.1 A_r} - 1}$ | $A_r$ = passband ripple dB |
| 12 | Pole radius Butterworth | $r = \omega_c$ | Always on circle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Butterworth is preferred in **audio and instrumentation** where flat passband matters. Chebyshev is used in **RF and communication** where sharp transition is more important than passband flatness. Bessel is used in **pulse and data transmission** where waveform preservation (linear phase) is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Butterworth Pole Locations

We start with the magnitude-squared condition:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = H(j\omega) H(-j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + (-1)^n \omega^{2n} / \omega_c^{2n}}$$

Replacing $j\omega$ with $s$:

$$H(s) H(-s) = \frac{1}{1 + (-1)^n (s/\omega_c)^{2n}}$$

The poles occur when the denominator equals zero:

$$1 + (-1)^n \left(\frac{s}{\omega_c}\right)^{2n} = 0$$

For $n$ even: $1 + (s/\omega_c)^{2n} = 0 \Rightarrow (s/\omega_c)^{2n} = -1$

For $n$ odd: $1 - (s/\omega_c)^{2n} = 0 \Rightarrow (s/\omega_c)^{2n} = 1$

In both cases, the combined $2n$ pole locations are:

$$\frac{s_k}{\omega_c} = e^{\,j\pi(2k-1)/(2n)}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2n$$

Equivalently, using the standard angle expression:

$$s_k = \omega_c \, e^{\,j\left[\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]}$$

For **stability**, we select only those poles with $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$ (lower half of the unit circle in $s$-plane). This gives exactly $n$ stable poles.

> [!NOTE]
> This proves the famous geometric fact: **Butterworth poles lie uniformly on a circle of radius $\omega_c$**, symmetrically placed about the real and imaginary axes.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example ‚ÄĒ Design of a 2nd-Order Butterworth LPF

**Problem:** Design a 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = 1$ kHz and unity DC gain. Use the Sallen-Key (VCVS) topology.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the normalized Butterworth polynomial.**
For $n = 2$, the standard 2nd-order Butterworth denominator polynomial is:

$$D(s) = s^2 + \sqrt{2} \, s + 1$$

Comparing to the standard 2nd-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_0 s + \omega_0^2$:

$$\omega_0 = 1 \text{ rad/s (normalized)}, \qquad 2\zeta = \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow \zeta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

So the **damping factor** is $\zeta = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and **quality factor** $Q = 1/(2\zeta) = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ ‚ÄĒ the classic **Butterworth $Q$**.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply frequency scaling to get desired $f_c$.**

$$k_f = \frac{\omega_c}{1} = 2\pi \times 1000 = 2000\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

We will choose component values satisfying $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}$ at $f_c = 1$ kHz.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Use the Sallen-Key (equal-component) design.**

For unity-gain Sallen-Key LPF with equal resistors and capacitors:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{and} \quad Q = \frac{1}{3 - K}$$

For Butterworth $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{3 - K} \Rightarrow 3 - K = \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$$

where $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_b}{R_a}$ is the non-inverting gain of the op-amp.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Pick practical component values.**
Choose $C = C_1 = C_2 = 10$ nF (standard value).

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 15.915 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use $R = 15.9$ k$\Omega$ (E-96 standard: 15.8 k$\Omega$ or 16 k$\Omega$).

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Choose feedback resistors for $K = 1.586$.**

$$K = 1 + \frac{R_b}{R_a} = 1.586 \Rightarrow \frac{R_b}{R_a} = 0.586$$

Choose $R_a = 10$ k$\Omega$, then $R_b = 5.86$ k$\Omega$ (use 5.76 k$\Omega$ E-96 standard).

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Verify using transfer function.**

$$H(s) = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$$

Substituting $K = 1.586$ and $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 1000$:

$$H(s) = \frac{1.586 \, \omega_0^2}{s^2 + 1.414 \, \omega_0 s + \omega_0^2} = \frac{1.586 \, \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2} \, \omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$$

This matches the 2nd-order Butterworth polynomial exactly. **Design complete.**

---

## 3.3 Frequency Transformation Worked Example ‚ÄĒ LPF to HPF

**Problem:** Convert the normalized 2nd-order Butterworth LPF $H_{LP}(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2} s + 1}$ into an HPF with cutoff $\omega_c$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply transformation $s \to \omega_c / s$:**

$$H_{HP}(s) = \left. H_{LP}(s) \right|_{s \to \omega_c / s}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute:**

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{\omega_c}{s}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2} \left(\dfrac{\omega_c}{s}\right) + 1}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\dfrac{\omega_c^2}{s^2} + \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\omega_c}{s} + 1}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^2 / \omega_c^2$:**

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\omega_c \, s + \omega_c^2}$$

This is the **standard 2nd-order HPF polynomial**, where $\omega_0 = \omega_c$ and the damping factor is preserved as $\zeta = 1/\sqrt{2}$. The numerator $s^2$ gives the **two zeros at $s=0$** that are characteristic of a high-pass response.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Butterworth Coefficients Table

Below is the **standard Butterworth denominator polynomial coefficients** (coefficient of $s^k$ for normalized $\omega_c = 1$):

| Order $n$ | Polynomial $D(s)$ |
|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | $s + 1$ |
| 2 | $s^2 + 1.4142 s + 1$ |
| 3 | $s^3 + 2.0000 s^2 + 2.0000 s + 1$ |
| 4 | $s^4 + 2.6131 s^3 + 3.4142 s^2 + 2.6131 s + 1$ |
| 5 | $s^5 + 3.2361 s^4 + 5.2361 s^3 + 5.2361 s^2 + 3.2361 s + 1$ |
| 6 | $s^6 + 3.8637 s^5 + 7.4641 s^4 + 9.1416 s^3 + 7.4641 s^2 + 3.8637 s + 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any order $n$, the coefficients satisfy the **palindromic (mirror) symmetry property** because Butterworth polynomials are **self-reciprocal**: if $D(s)$ has roots $r_k$, then $1/r_k$ are also roots. This guarantees that the HPF, BPF, BSF derived from the same prototype also have stable, realizable forms.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Sallen-Key LPF Transfer Function

Consider the equal-component Sallen-Key LPF: $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$, op-amp non-inverting gain $K$.

KCL at the non-inverting input and analysis of the feedback network gives:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$$

where $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$.

Comparing with the general 2nd-order denominator $s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} s + \omega_0^2$:

$$\frac{\omega_0}{Q} = (3-K)\omega_0 \Rightarrow Q = \frac{1}{3-K}$$

> [!TIP]
> For $K = 1$ (unity gain): $Q = 1/2$ ‚ÄĒ this is a **Bessel-like** response.
> For $K = 1.586$: $Q = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ ‚ÄĒ **Butterworth**.
> For $K = 2$: $Q = 1$ ‚ÄĒ critically damped.
> For $K \to 3$: $Q \to \infty$ ‚ÄĒ **oscillator**! (instability).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Generic Filter Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Gather Specifications] --> B[Step 2: Choose Approximation Type]
    B --> C[Step 3: Calculate Filter Order n]
    C --> D[Step 4: Look Up Normalized LPF Polynomial]
    D --> E{Step 5: Choose Topology}
    E --> F[Passive RLC]
    E --> G[Active Sallen-Key]
    E --> H[Active Multiple-Feedback]
    E --> I[Switched-Capacitor]
    F --> J[Step 6: Apply Frequency Transformation if needed]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step 7: Magnitude and Frequency Scaling]
    K --> L[Step 8: Select Standard Component Values]
    L --> M[Step 9: Verify H jw and Phase]
    M --> N[Step 10: Simulate SPICE and Build]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is the **canonical KTU lab workflow** for any filter design ‚ÄĒ pass through all 10 gates to ensure a correct, stable, and realizable filter.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Filter Approximation Trade-offs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Required Filter] --> B{Passband Flatness}
    A --> C{Transition Steepness}
    A --> D{Phase Linearity}

    B -->|High| E[Butterworth]
    C -->|High| F[Chebyshev]
    D -->|High| G[Bessel]

    E --> H[Audio and Instrumentation]
    F --> I[RF and Communications]
    G --> J[Pulse and Data Transmission]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Block ‚ÄĒ Sallen-Key Low-Pass Filter (2nd Order)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin[Vin] --> R1[R1] --> NodeA[Node A]
    NodeA --> C1[C1] --> Gnd1[Ground]
    NodeA --> R2[R2] --> NodeB[Node B]
    NodeB --> C2[C2] --> Gnd2[Ground]
    NodeB --> OpampPos[Op-Amp + Terminal]
    OpampOut[Op-Amp Output Vout] --> Ra[Ra] --> OpampNeg[Op-Amp - Terminal]
    OpampNeg --> Rb[Rb] --> Gnd3[Ground]
    OpampOut --> Vout[Vout Final Output]
```

**Component Roles in the Sallen-Key LPF:**

| Element | Function |
|---------|----------|
| $R_1, R_2, C_1$ | Form the input RC ladder that sets the natural frequency $\omega_0$ |
| $C_2$ | Feedback capacitor that sets the damping / $Q$-factor |
| Op-amp | Provides gain $K = 1 + R_b/R_a$ and isolates stages |
| $R_a, R_b$ | Set the gain $K$ which controls $Q$ |

---

## 4.4 Pole Location Diagram (Butterworth, n = 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph sPlane["s-Plane: 4th-Order Butterworth Poles on Unit Circle"]
        P1[Pole 1: -0.383 + j0.924]
        P2[Pole 2: -0.924 + j0.383]
        P3[Pole 3: -0.924 - j0.383]
        P4[Pole 4: -0.383 - j0.924]
        Rej[Real Axis]
        Imj[Imaginary Axis]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the conjugate symmetry: $P_1 = P_4^*$ and $P_2 = P_3^*$. All four poles lie exactly on the unit circle ($r = 1$) because $\omega_c = 1$ in the normalized prototype. For denormalized designs, scale the circle radius to $\omega_c$.

---

## 4.5 Architecture ‚ÄĒ Cascaded Filter Realization

For higher-order filters, second-order sections (SOS) are **cascaded**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin] --> B1[First-Order Section 1] --> B2[Second-Order Section 2] --> B3[Second-Order Section 3] --> C[Vout]
    B1 -.-> D1[Provides one real pole]
    B2 -.-> D2[Provides complex pole pair 1]
    B3 -.-> D3[Provides complex pole pair 2]
```

> [!TIP]
> For a 5th-order filter, use **one 1st-order section** (for the single real pole at $-\omega_c$) plus **two 2nd-order sections** (for the two complex-conjugate pole pairs). Each section is implemented separately to avoid loading effects and to allow independent $Q$ tuning.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question A1, 3 Marks]:**
> **Q:** Define the terms *passband*, *stopband*, and *transition band* of an electric filter with a neat magnitude response sketch.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> - **Passband:** The range of frequencies over which the filter transmits the input signal with attenuation less than a specified maximum $A_p$ (typically 0‚Äď3 dB).
> - **Stopband:** The range of frequencies over which the filter attenuates the input signal by at least a specified minimum $A_s$ (typically 40‚Äď60 dB).
> - **Transition band:** The frequency range between the passband edge $\omega_p$ and the stopband edge $\omega_s$, where the response transitions from $A_p$ to $A_s$.
>
> **Magnitude Sketch:** (A flat horizontal line at 0 dB from 0 to $\omega_p$, then a sloping line descending at $-20n$ dB/decade through $\omega_s$, and a flat line at $-A_s$ beyond $\omega_s$.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question A2, 3 Marks]:**
> **Q:** List any three approximations used in filter design and state one distinguishing feature of each.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Butterworth:** Maximally flat magnitude response in the passband (no ripple); all poles on a circle in the $s$-plane.
> 2. **Chebyshev (Type-I):** Equiripple in the passband, monotonic in the stopband; gives sharper cutoff than Butterworth for the same order.
> 3. **Bessel (Thomson):** Maximally flat group delay (linear phase); best pulse response, but the slowest transition.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *[KTU University Exam - Model Question B1]*

**(a)** Derive the magnitude-squared response of an $n$th-order Butterworth low-pass filter and show that the poles lie on a circle of radius $\omega_c$ in the $s$-plane. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a Butterworth LPF with $A_p = 3$ dB at $f_p = 2$ kHz and $A_s = 30$ dB at $f_s = 5$ kHz, determine the required filter order $n$ and the corresponding cutoff frequency. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

Starting from the definition:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^{2n}}$$

Replace $j\omega$ with $s$ to find poles where denominator = 0:

$$1 + (-1)^n (s/\omega_c)^{2n} = 0 \Rightarrow (s/\omega_c)^{2n} = (-1)^{n+1}$$

The $2n$ roots on the complex $s/\omega_c$ plane are:

$$s_k/\omega_c = e^{\,j\pi(2k-1+n)/(2n)}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2n$$

$$s_k = \omega_c \, e^{\,j\left[\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right]}$$

Since $\vert e^{j\theta} \vert = 1$, every pole has magnitude $\vert s_k \vert = \omega_c$, which proves they all lie on a circle of radius $\omega_c$ centered at the origin. For stability we retain only the $n$ poles in the **left half-plane** ($\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$).

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

Apply the order formula:

$$n \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s}-1}{10^{0.1 A_p}-1}}\right)}{\log_{10}(\omega_s/\omega_p)}$$

Substitute $A_p = 3$ dB, $A_s = 30$ dB, $\omega_s/\omega_p = 5/2 = 2.5$:

$$10^{0.1 \times 3} - 1 = 10^{0.3} - 1 = 0.9953$$

$$10^{0.1 \times 30} - 1 = 10^{3} - 1 = 999$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{999}{0.9953}} = \sqrt{1003.7} \approx 31.68$$

$$\log_{10}(31.68) = 1.5008$$

$$\log_{10}(2.5) = 0.3979$$

$$n \ge \frac{1.5008}{0.3979} \approx 3.77 \Rightarrow n = 4$$

Choose $n = 4$ (next integer ‚Č• 3.77).

The 3 dB cutoff $\omega_c$ is found by ensuring $\vert H(j\omega_p)\vert$ corresponds to $A_p = 3$ dB:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega_p/\omega_c)^{2n}}} = 10^{-A_p/20} = 10^{-0.15} = 0.7079$$

$$(\omega_p/\omega_c)^{2n} = (1/0.7079)^2 - 1 = 1 - 1 = 1 - 1 = 1$$

$$\omega_p/\omega_c = 1^{1/2n} = 1 \Rightarrow \omega_c = \omega_p = 2\pi \times 2000 = 4000\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

So $f_c = 2$ kHz (which is the 3 dB cutoff, since $A_p = 3$ dB).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *[KTU University Exam - Model Question B2, Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Sallen-Key second-order low-pass filter. Derive its transfer function and the expressions for $\omega_0$ and $Q$ when $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a 2nd-order Butterworth Sallen-Key LPF with unity DC gain and cutoff $f_c = 5$ kHz. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

The **Sallen-Key (VCVS) LPF** uses a non-inverting op-amp with two RC stages at its input.

Writing node equations at the junction of $R_2$ and $C_2$ (call it $V_1$, the op-amp non-inverting input) and at the output node, and using the op-amp gain $K = 1 + R_b/R_a$:

$$\frac{V_i - V_1}{R_1 + 1/sC_1} = (V_1 - V_2) s C_2$$

$$V_o = K V_1, \quad V_2 = V_o = K V_1$$

Solving these simultaneous equations yields the standard result:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{K \, \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (3-K)\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$$

where $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ for the equal-component case.

Comparing with $s^2 + (\omega_0/Q) s + \omega_0^2$:

$$\frac{1}{Q} = 3 - K \Rightarrow Q = \frac{1}{3-K}$$

**For unity gain** ($K = 1$): $Q = 1/2$.
**For Butterworth** ($Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$): $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Required parameters: $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 5000 = 31{,}416$ rad/s.

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Choose $C = 10$ nF (standard value).

Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute $R$:

$$R = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = \frac{1}{31{,}416 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = 3183 \text{ }\Omega \approx 3.18 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Choose $R_a = 10$ k$\Omega$. Then:

$$R_b = (K - 1) R_a = (1.586 - 1)(10\text{k}) = 0.586 \times 10\text{k} = 5.86 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use standard E-96 values: $R = 3.16$ k$\Omega$ (or 3.18 k$\Omega$), $R_b = 5.76$ k$\Omega$ or 5.90 k$\Omega$.

**Final component list:**
- $R_1 = R_2 = 3.18$ k$\Omega$
- $C_1 = C_2 = 10$ nF
- $R_a = 10$ k$\Omega$
- $R_b = 5.86$ k$\Omega$
- Op-amp: LM741 or TL081 (single-supply 9 V)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚ÄĒ Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to scale the prototype:** Designing with $\omega_c = 1$ rad/s and submitting it as the final answer. Always apply frequency scaling and round to E-12/E-24 standard values.
> 2. **Confusing $K$ and $K-1$:** $K = 1 + R_b/R_a$ is the closed-loop gain; the ratio $R_b/R_a$ alone is $K-1$. Many students write $R_b/R_a = 1.586$, which is wrong.
> 3. **Using Chebyshev formula for Butterworth or vice versa:** The two approximations have **completely different** pole locations. Make sure to use the right table.
> 4. **Not drawing the op-amp feedback path:** Sallen-Key requires the $R_a, R_b$ divider on the inverting terminal. Omitting it loses at least 1‚Äď2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the stopband/passband ripples in dB:** The examiner expects $A_p$ and $A_s$ explicitly quoted, not just "the filter should be sharp."
> 6. **Using inductors in audio-range LPF designs:** For $f_c < 10$ kHz, inductors are bulky, lossy, and expensive. Always prefer active (op-amp + RC) designs in this range ‚ÄĒ the examiner will deduct marks for impractical $L$ values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Bullets Before the Exam:**

- [ ] A filter is a **frequency-selective** network. The four canonical types are LPF, HPF, BPF, and BSF.
- [ ] The **Butterworth response** is $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = 1/\sqrt{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^{2n}}$.
- [ ] **Roll-off** beyond cutoff is **$20n$ dB/decade** for an $n$th-order filter.
- [ ] The **filter order formula** uses $\log_{10}$ of the attenuation ratio divided by $\log_{10}$ of the frequency ratio ‚ÄĒ always take the **ceiling** of the result.
- [ ] **Butterworth poles** lie on a circle of radius $\omega_c$ in the $s$-plane; the 2nd-order polynomial is always $s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\omega_c s + \omega_c^2$.
- [ ] **Frequency transformations:** $s \to s/\omega_c$ (LP), $\omega_c/s$ (HP), $(s^2+\omega_0^2)/(Bs)$ (BP), $Bs/(s^2+\omega_0^2)$ (BS).
- [ ] **Sallen-Key (VCVS)** is the most common active topology: $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ and $Q = 1/(3-K)$ for equal components.
- [ ] For **Butterworth** in Sallen-Key: $K = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.586$, $Q = 1/\sqrt{2}$.
- [ ] **Magnitude scaling** multiplies $R$ and $L$ by $k_m$ and divides $C$ by $k_m$.
- [ ] **Frequency scaling** divides $L$ and $C$ by $k_f$ and leaves $R$ unchanged.
- [ ] **Active filters** are preferred for $f_c < 100$ kHz; **passive LC filters** are preferred for $f_c > 1$ MHz (RF range).
- [ ] **Cascaded SOS** (second-order sections) are used to realize higher-order filters ‚ÄĒ never implement a 4th-order filter as a single 4th-order network.
- [ ] Always **simulate in SPICE / LTspice / Multisim** before fabricating ‚ÄĒ practical op-amp GBW limits and parasitic capacitances will shift $\omega_0$.
- [ ] The **op-amp GBW** must be at least $20\times$ to $100\times$ the filter cutoff to avoid phase errors.
- [ ] **Unity-gain buffer (voltage follower)** at the end of cascaded sections prevents loading ‚ÄĒ the examiner will appreciate this mention.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"Butterworth: **B**eautifully **F**lat"* (Maximally Flat Magnitude), *"Chebyshev: **C**ut sharp, but **R**ippled"* (Ripple in Passband), *"Bessel: **B**est for **P**ulses"* (Linear Phase).
